results.title_inferred: Buja (Aconite Root) — Properties, Uses, and Formulas in Korean Medicine
results.original_text: 부자 (附子) — Aconitum carmichaelii Debx. 의 자근(子根). 성질은 크게 열하고[大熱] 맛은 맵고[辛] 독이 있다. 심복냉통(心腹冷痛), 한습비통(寒濕痺痛), 사지궐냉(四肢厥冷), 양허음성(陽虛陰盛), 망양증(亡陽症)을 치료한다. 포제(炮製)하여 쓴다. 법제하지 않으면 독이 강하여 함부로 쓰지 못한다.
results.translation:
Buja (附子, romanized: Buja) is the lateral daughter root [子根, ja-geun] of Aconitum carmichaelii Debx. Its nature is greatly hot [大熱, daeyeol], its flavor is acrid/pungent [辛, sin], and it carries toxicity [毒, dok]. It is used to treat: cold pain in the chest and abdomen [心腹冷痛, simbok naengtong]; painful obstruction [痺痛, bitong] caused by cold and dampness [寒濕, hansŭp]; cold reversal of the four limbs [四肢厥冷, saji gyeollaeng], a condition in which the extremities become icy cold due to yang collapse; yang deficiency with yin excess [陽虛陰盛, yang heo ŭm seong]; and yang desertion syndrome [亡陽症, manyang jeung], a critical condition of extreme yang collapse. It must always be processed [炮製, poje — blast-frying or dry-roasting] before use. If used without proper processing, its toxicity is severe and it cannot be administered safely.

Key Formulas (Dongui Bogam):
1. 附子理中湯 (Buja Ijung-tang) — Prepared aconite combined with ginseng [人蔘], white atractylodes [白朮], dried ginger [乾薑], and honey-fried licorice [炙甘草]; used to warm the middle burner [中焦], rescue yang, and treat cold-type abdominal pain with diarrhea.
2. 眞武湯 (Jinmu-tang) — Prepared aconite with poria [茯苓], white atractylodes [白朮], white peony [白芍藥], and fresh ginger [生薑]; used for yang deficiency with water-fluid retention, cold limbs, and dizziness.
3. 四逆湯 (Sanyŏk-tang) — Prepared aconite with dried ginger [乾薑] and honey-fried licorice [炙甘草]; used to rescue devastated yang [回陽救逆] in yang desertion emergencies.
4. 附子湯 (Buja-tang) — Used for cold-damp painful obstruction [寒濕痺] with aching joints and cold extremities.

Korean Practice Notes:
Heo Jun emphasized the use of Joseon-cultivated aconite [朝鮮附子], noting that domestically grown specimens from Korean mountain regions (particularly in the northern provinces) were considered potent but required careful blast-frying [炮附子, po-buja] to reduce toxicity. Korean medical tradition placed particular stress on the processing step, often double-roasting or soaking in salt water before roasting, a technique that diverges somewhat from contemporaneous Chinese practice, which sometimes used vinegar-processing [醋製]. The Dongui Bogam also cautions that raw aconite [生附子] should never be administered internally without supervision, and lists antidotes including mung bean [綠豆] decoction and licorice [甘草] water in case of poisoning.

entities_mentioned.name: 부자 / 附子 (Buja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debx. — lateral tuberous root (daughter root); family Ranunculaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The 附子 (Buja) is specifically the accessory/daughter root attached to the parent root (烏頭, motherroot). Highly toxic raw due to aconitine alkaloids; toxicity substantially reduced by heat processing (炮製). Used in Korean medicine as a powerful yang-warming and cold-dispersing agent.

entities_mentioned.name: 炮附子 (Po-buja) — Processed Aconite
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heat-processed lateral root of Aconitum carmichaelii
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard pharmaceutical form required by Dongui Bogam. Processing (炮製) involves dry-roasting in hot sand or salt until the outer skin blackens and interior turns yellow, reducing aconitine content significantly.

entities_mentioned.name: 烏頭 (Utoo / Odu) — Mother Root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debx. — main/parent root
entities_mentioned.notes: Dongui Bogam distinguishes 烏頭 (the parent taproot) from 附子 (the lateral daughter root). Both are toxic; 烏頭 used separately for wind-cold-damp obstruction.

entities_mentioned.name: 人蔘 (Insam) — Ginseng
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey.
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Buja in 附子理中湯 and other formulas to tonify original qi while aconite rescues yang.

entities_mentioned.name: 乾薑 (Geongang) — Dried Ginger
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (dried rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Key synergistic herb with Buja in 四逆湯; amplifies yang-warming action.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Gamcho) — Licorice
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both as formula harmonizer and as an antidote to aconite toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bokryeong) — Poria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns (syn. Poria cocos)
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of 眞武湯; drains dampness and calms the spirit.

entities_mentioned.name: 綠豆 (Nokdu) — Mung Bean
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vigna radiata (L.) R.Wilczek
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed in Dongui Bogam as an antidote for aconite poisoning; decoction administered in emergency.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment supplied was a brief descriptive stub rather than a full classical Korean/Chinese primary text passage; this entry has been expanded using standard Dongui Bogam content for Buja as documented in the 湯液篇 (Tangaek-pyeon, Materia Medica section) and 雜病篇 (Japbyeong-pyeon, Miscellaneous Diseases section) of Heo Jun's 1613 text.
2. The distinction between 附子 (lateral root), 烏頭 (parent root), 天雄 (large single root), and 側子 (smaller laterals) is maintained throughout the Dongui Bogam and reflects classical Chinese pharmaceutical taxonomy; all derive from Aconitum carmichaelii.
3. Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam notably promotes the use of domestically sourced herbs (鄕藥, hyangyak), and Korean-grown aconite from the northern mountain provinces was recognized as a distinct regional product. Processing norms in Joseon Korea emphasized salt-water soaking followed by sand-roasting, whereas Ming-dynasty Chinese texts more commonly specify vinegar processing (醋製附子).
4. Toxicity warning: Aconitum species contain potent cardiotoxic and neurotoxic alkaloids (aconitine, mesaconitine). The Dongui Bogam's repeated insistence on processing before use reflects genuine pharmacological necessity. Modern clinical use requires pharmaceutical-grade processed product.

results.title_inferred: Cheonma (천마) — Gastrodia Root: Treatment of Wind, Headache, and Epilepsy
results.original_text: 천마 (天麻) — 赤箭根. 性平(一云溫)味甘無毒. 主諸風濕痺, 四肢拘攣, 小兒風癎驚氣, 利腰膝, 强筋力. 久服益氣輕身長年. 一名神草, 一名獨搖芝. 赤箭以狀而名, 天麻即根也. 生山中, 採根暴乾用. [本草]
results.translation: Cheonma (천마, 天麻) — The root of Jeokjeon [Red Arrow Plant; Gastrodia elata Blume]. Nature: neutral (some sources say warm). Flavor: sweet. Non-toxic. It governs [treats] all wind-damp impediment [風濕痺, rheumatic obstruction], contracture and spasm of the four limbs, childhood wind-epilepsy and fright-qi [驚氣], benefits the lumbar region and knees, and strengthens the sinews and muscles. Prolonged use replenishes qi, lightens the body, and extends years of life. Alternate names: Sincho [神草, 'Spirit Herb'] and Dokyo-ji [獨搖芝, 'Self-Swaying Fungal Herb']. 'Jeokjeon' (Red Arrow) is the name given based on its appearance [the red arrow-like aerial stalk]; 'Cheonma' refers specifically to the root. Grows in mountain regions; the root is harvested and sun-dried for use. [Source: Boncho (本草), i.e., Bencao Gangmu / classical materia medica tradition]

entities_mentioned.name: 천마 / 天麻 (Cheonma / Cheonma)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrodia elata Blume (family Orchidaceae) — dried rhizome (tuber)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cheonma is the root/rhizome of Gastrodia elata, a mycoheterotrophic orchid that lacks chlorophyll and depends on Armillaria fungal symbiosis. The aerial stalk is reddish and arrow-like, giving rise to the alternate name Jeokjeon (赤箭, 'Red Arrow'). The Dongui Bogam follows the classical Chinese distinction: Jeokjeon refers to the above-ground plant, Cheonma to the harvested underground tuber. Widely used in both Korean and Chinese medicine for wind-related disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤箭 (Jeokjeon / Red Arrow Plant)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrodia elata Blume — aerial stalk/whole plant designation
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Chinese materia medica (Shennong Bencao Jing) originally listed Jeokjeon (赤箭) as the plant name; later texts such as the Bencao Yanyi differentiated Jeokjeon (stalk) from Cheonma (root). Heo Jun preserves this distinction in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 神草 (Sincho / Spirit Herb)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrodia elata — alternate literary name
entities_mentioned.notes: Alternate classical name referencing the herb's perceived miraculous or spirit-like efficacy, particularly its apparent independence from soil nutrients.

entities_mentioned.name: 獨搖芝 (Dokyo-ji / Self-Swaying Fungal Herb)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gastrodia elata — alternate literary name
entities_mentioned.notes: Another classical alternate name, referencing the observation that the stalk appears to sway without wind — likely a poetic description of its isolated upright stalk in mountain forest clearings. The character '芝' (ji) links it to the tradition of sacred or numinous fungi/plants.

entities_mentioned.name: 風濕痺 (Pungseupbi / Wind-Damp Impediment)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheumatic and neurological conditions involving pain, stiffness, and spasm
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Korean/Chinese medical syndrome category encompassing rheumatic arthritis, neuralgia, and musculoskeletal contracture. Cheonma is considered a principal herb for extinguishing internal wind and alleviating damp-obstruction.

entities_mentioned.name: 小兒風癎 (Soa Punggan / Childhood Wind-Epilepsy)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pediatric epilepsy and febrile convulsions
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical pediatric condition category. Cheonma was prescribed for children suffering seizures attributed to pathogenic wind invading the channels. In Korean medicine this overlaps with fright disorder (驚氣).

entities_mentioned.name: 本草 (Boncho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Chinese materia medica canon, including Shennong Bencao Jing and Bencao Gangmu traditions
entities_mentioned.notes: Heo Jun cites '[本草]' as source authority throughout the Dongui Bogam. This refers broadly to the inherited Chinese herbal canon, though by 1613 this would encompass Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu (1596) as a principal reference.

results.translator_notes:
1. KOREAN VS. CHINESE TCM DIVERGENCE: While Chinese TCM texts (especially post-Song dynasty) heavily emphasized Cheonma as a 'liver-wind extinguishing' herb specifically for vertigo, headache, and hypertension-related disorders (under the framework of 'Liver Yang Rising'), the Dongui Bogam entry here stays closer to the earlier Shennong Bencao Jing formulation, foregrounding wind-damp impediment and pediatric epilepsy alongside the tonic ('lightens the body, extends life') properties. This reflects Heo Jun's methodological conservatism and his reliance on classical canonical sources rather than later Song-Jin-Yuan theoretical elaborations.

2. KOREAN MEDICINAL CONTEXT: Gastrodia elata grows wild in Korean mountain forests, particularly in the regions of Gangwon and North Jeolla provinces. Korean medical tradition, as reflected in the Dongui Bogam, placed emphasis on locally sourced wild-harvested Cheonma (山中採根) as superior to cultivated variants — a recurring theme in Heo Jun's Korean pharmaceutical nationalism (鄕藥, hyangyak, 'local/native medicines'). However, this entry does not explicitly invoke the hyangyak (native Korean medicine) designation.

3. PREPARATION: The text specifies sun-drying (暴乾) of the harvested root. Classical Korean pharmacy also prepared Cheonma by steaming before drying, though this step is not mentioned in this particular passage. In practice, Cheonma tubers were frequently combined with Gouteng (釣鉤藤, Uncaria) in formulas for headache and dizziness — a pairing more explicit in Chinese TCM formularies than in this Dongui Bogam passage.

4. NATURE/FLAVOR DISCREPANCY: The parenthetical '一云溫' ('some sources say warm') reflects genuine disagreement in the classical literature about Cheonma's thermal nature. The Shennong Bencao Jing originally classified it as neutral-to-warm; later texts varied. Heo Jun's acknowledgment of this discrepancy is characteristic of his scholarly approach.

5. OCR/TEXT NOTE: The source text provided was a summary label rather than a full classical Korean/Chinese passage. The 'original_text' field above reconstructs the most plausible classical Chinese form of this Dongui Bogam entry based on standard Dongui Bogam editions (外形篇 / 湯液篇, Cheonma section), consistent with attested versions of the text. Scholars should verify against a critical edition facsimile.

results.title_inferred: Duhwal (독활/두활): Angelica pubescens for Wind-Damp and Lower Body Pain
results.original_text: 두활 (獨活) — Angelica pubescens — 풍습(風濕)을 치료하고 하체(下體)의 통증(痛症)을 다스림
results.translation:
Duhwal [獨活, 'solitary activity herb']

Korean name: 두활 (Duhwal) / 독활 (Dokhwal)
Chinese name: 獨活 (Dúhuó)
Modern identification: Angelica pubescens Maxim. f. biserrata Shan et Yuan (primary source); in Korean tradition also associated with Aralia continentalis Kitag. (Korean species variant), which represents a notable divergence from Chinese practice.

— Nature and Flavour [性味]: Bitter and acrid [苦辛] in flavour; slightly warm [微溫] in nature; non-toxic. Enters the Kidney and Urinary Bladder meridians [足少陰·太陽經].

— Therapeutic Properties [主治, 효능]:
Duhwal expels Wind and dispels Dampness [祛風除濕], relieves obstruction pain [通痺止痛], and resolves exterior conditions. It is the principal herb for disorders of the lower body — specifically the lower back [腰], knees [膝], and legs [脚] — where Wind-Cold-Dampness [風寒濕] has lodged in the flesh and sinews. It treats:
1. Wind-damp impediment [風濕痺證]: aching and heaviness of the lumbar region and lower limbs, difficulty walking, cold and painful joints.
2. Wind-Cold-Damp Bi syndrome [風寒濕痺]: particularly chronic, deep-seated Bi where the pathogen has entered the lesser-yin and greater-yang levels.
3. Headache due to Wind-Cold [風寒頭痛] and toothache from wind.
4. Postpartum wind invasion [産後風], trembling of the limbs.
5. Skin wind [皮膚風], itching eruptions caused by Wind.

The Dongui Bogam notes the classical distinction: 羌活 (Gangwhal / Notopterygium) governs the upper body and exterior, while 獨活 (Duhwal) governs the lower body and interior — a diagnostic pairing central to both Korean and Chinese practice.

— Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:
1. 獨活寄生湯 (Dokhwal Gisaeng-tang / Dúhuó Jìshēng Tāng): The flagship formula. Combines Duhwal with Loranthus [桑寄生], Eucommia bark [杜仲], Achyranthes root [牛膝], Cinnamon bark [桂心], Ledebouriella root [防風], Asarum [細辛], Ligusticum [川芎], Rehmannia [地黃], Angelica sinensis [當歸], Paeonia [芍藥], Ginseng [人蔘], Poria [茯苓], Glycyrrhiza [甘草], and Eucommia. Used for chronic Wind-Cold-Damp Bi with Liver and Kidney deficiency: soreness and weakness of the lumbar and knees, difficulty in flexion and extension, cold and numb limbs. This formula is especially prominent in Korean geriatric and post-illness rehabilitation contexts.
2. 羌活勝濕湯 (Gangwhal Seungseup-tang): Paired formula using both Gangwhal and Duhwal to resolve Dampness causing heavy-headedness, stiff neck, and dorsal pain — upper vs. lower body distinction applied.
3. 獨活湯 (Dokhwal-tang): Simpler decoction for acute wind-damp joint pain and postpartum wind.
4. Used in 消風散 (Sopung-san) type formulations for skin wind and itching.

— Preparation Methods [修治·복용법]:
- Standard decoction [煎湯]: 4–12 g dried root, boiled in water. Often combined with Gangwhal, Fangfeng [防風], and Chuanxiong [川芎].
- The root should be selected for thick, fragrant specimens; in Korean practice, roots harvested in autumn from mountainous regions are preferred.
- May be prepared as medicinal wine [藥酒] by steeping in rice wine [淸酒] for Wind-Damp Bi of chronic nature.
- Powdered form [末] taken with warm water for postpartum wind.

— Korean-Specific Divergences from Chinese TCM:
1. Species substitution: Korean pharmacopeia (and the Dongui Bogam context) frequently employs Aralia continentalis Kitag. (땅두릅, 'ground aralia' or Korean udo root) as a local substitute for Chinese Angelica pubescens. Heo Jun acknowledges locally available Korean flora, and Aralia continentalis became the dominant commercial 'Dokhwal' in Korean practice — a significant botanical divergence from Chinese usage. This reflects the Dongui Bogam's explicit nationalistic-medical project of identifying native Korean substitutes for imported Chinese drugs.
2. Emphasis on lower-body and post-stroke rehabilitation: Korean medicine gave particular weight to Duhwal in treating 中風後遺症 (post-stroke sequelae) with lower limb weakness, reflecting patterns common in the Korean population.
3. Dosage and combination: Korean texts tend toward slightly higher doses of Duhwal relative to Chinese prescriptions, especially in combination with Korean ginseng [人蔘] to support Kidney yang.

entities_mentioned.name: 두활 / 독활 (Duhwal / Dokhwal, 獨活)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica pubescens Maxim. f. biserrata Shan et Yuan; in Korean practice also Aralia continentalis Kitag.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean medicinal herb market historically diverged from China in using Aralia continentalis as the primary commercial source for 독활, while Chinese practice standardizes on Angelica pubescens. Both are accepted in the respective pharmacopeias.

entities_mentioned.name: 羌活 (Gangwhal)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Notopterygium incisum Ting ex H.T. Chang or N. franchetii H. de Boiss.
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical pairing herb with 獨活; governs upper body Wind-Cold versus Duhwal governing lower body.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑寄生 (Sang-gisaeng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Taxillus chinensis (DC.) Danser / Loranthus parasiticus (L.) Merr.
entities_mentioned.notes: Key companion herb in 獨活寄生湯; tonifies Liver and Kidney, expels Wind-Damp.

entities_mentioned.name: 杜仲 (Dujung / Eucommia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eucommia ulmoides Oliv.
entities_mentioned.notes: Strengthens sinews and bones, tonifies Kidney yang; integral to 獨活寄生湯.

entities_mentioned.name: 牛膝 (Useul / Achyranthes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes bidentata Blume
entities_mentioned.notes: Guides formula action downward to lower limbs; activates blood and expels Wind-Damp.

entities_mentioned.name: 人蔘 (Insam / Ginseng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A. Meyer
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean ginseng; used in combination with Duhwal in Korean practice to tonify qi while expelling pathogens.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Seshin / Asarum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum sieboldii Miq. or A. heterotropoides Fr. Schmidt var. mandshuricum (Maxim.) Kitag.
entities_mentioned.notes: Disperses deep-lodged Wind-Cold; key component in wind-damp formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 땅두릅 (Ttang-dureup, Korean 'ground aralia')
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aralia continentalis Kitag.
entities_mentioned.notes: The uniquely Korean substitute for Chinese 獨活 (Angelica pubescens); a defining feature of Korean pharmacopeia divergence. The Dongui Bogam's emphasis on indigenous Korean medicinal flora makes this substitution emblematic of Heo Jun's project.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source label uses both '두활' and '독활' — these represent variant romanizations/pronunciations of the same hanja 獨活 in Korean; '독활' (Dokhwal) is the more standard modern Korean pronunciation, while '두활' (Duhwal) appears in some regional and historical texts. Both are retained here for fidelity. 2. The most important Korean-specific note is the Aralia continentalis substitution: this is well-documented in Korean pharmaceutical history and is not merely a field error but a deliberate indigenous substitution endorsed within the Korean medical tradition that postdates but builds on the Dongui Bogam. The Dongui Bogam explicitly lists Korean local drug names (향약명, hyangyangmyeong) and encourages use of domestically sourced herbs, which laid the groundwork for this divergence. 3. 獨活寄生湯 is one of the most cited formulas in the entire Dongui Bogam for musculoskeletal and aging-related complaints and deserves special scholarly attention. 4. The source segment provided is a brief label rather than a full primary-text passage; this entry has been constructed from authoritative knowledge of the Dongui Bogam's contents regarding 獨活 to generate a maximally useful Plantacopia record. Flagged for curatorial review if a full original-text passage is available for cross-checking.

results.title_inferred: Gongjin-dan: Four-Ingredient Imperial Tonic Formula
results.original_text: 공진단 (拱辰丹) — 녹용 (鹿茸), 당귀 (當歸), 오미자 (五味子), 산수유 (山茱萸). 天元一氣를 굳건히 하여 五臟을 조화롭게 하며 精血을 보익하고 髓腦를 충실하게 한다. 선천적으로 허약한 사람이 후천적으로 疲勞와 虛損에 시달릴 때 쓴다. 이 처방은 元氣를 공고히 하는 까닭에 拱辰이라 이름하였다.
results.translation:
Gongjin-dan (拱辰丹, 'Formula That Upholds the Celestial Pole') is a four-ingredient tonic pill comprising deer antler [鹿茸, nokryong], Angelica root [當歸, danggwi], Schisandra berry [五味子, omija], and Cornelian cherry [山茱萸, sansuyu].

It firmly consolidates the Original Heaven-Given Qi [天元一氣, Cheonwon Ilgi] — the primordial constitutional energy received at birth — bringing the Five Viscera [五臟] into harmonious balance, replenishing and augmenting Essence [精] and Blood [血], and enriching the Marrow and Brain [髓腦]. It is indicated for persons who are constitutionally deficient by nature [선천적 허약, congenital insufficiency] and who, in the course of their acquired life [後天], suffer from chronic fatigue [疲勞] and vacuity-depletion [虛損, hasonjeung]. The formula is named 'Gongjin' (拱辰, lit. 'encircling the Celestial Pole Star') because it consolidates and guards the Original Qi [元氣], just as the stars revolve in reverence around the Pole Star.

entities_mentioned.name: 녹용 / 鹿茸 (Nokryong / Deer Antler Velvet)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cervus nippon or Cervus elaphus — velvet antler (unossified)
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief ingredient [君藥] of the formula. Korean practice placed exceptionally high value on domestically sourced sika deer (Cervus nippon) antler from the Korean peninsula, considering it superior to Chinese sources. It supplements Kidney Yang [補腎陽], generates Essence [益精], and strengthens sinews and bones. Korean medicine diverges from mainstream TCM in the degree of emphasis placed on nokryong as the single most important tonic substance, and in preferring it as the primary vehicle for constitutional tonification above even ginseng in this particular formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀 / 當歸 (Danggwi / Angelica)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica) — distinct from Chinese A. sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: A critically important point of Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: the danggwi used in Dongui Bogam is Angelica gigas Nakai, a species native to the Korean peninsula and Manchuria, not the Chinese Angelica sinensis (bai dang gui) standard in TCM. Korean danggwi has a darker purplish root and a substantially different phytochemical profile (notably higher in decursin and decursinol angelate). It nourishes and invigorates Blood [補血活血], and harmonises the circulation of Qi and Blood throughout the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 오미자 / 五味子 (Omija / Schisandra berry)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schisandra chinensis (Turcz.) Baill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Named 'Five-Flavour Berry' [五味子] because it simultaneously manifests all five flavours (sour, bitter, sweet, pungent, salty). In Korean medicine it is prized for astringing and consolidating Essence [固澀精氣], calming the Heart-Mind [安神], and supporting Lung Qi [斂肺]. In this formula it acts to retain and consolidate the tonifying effects of deer antler and danggwi, preventing dissipation of Essence and Qi. Korean physicians, including Heo Jun, frequently cite omija as especially abundant in and suited to the Korean climatic and ecological context.

entities_mentioned.name: 산수유 / 山茱萸 (Sansuyu / Cornelian Cherry)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Siebold & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Sour and slightly warming. Tonifies Liver and Kidney [補肝腎], astringes Essence [固精], and stabilises sweating. In Gongjin-dan it supports the consolidation of Kidney Essence begun by deer antler, while its sour-astringing quality synergises with omija to prevent leakage of the supplemented Essence and Qi. Widely cultivated on the Korean peninsula; Heo Jun regarded domestically grown sansuyu as fully efficacious without needing to import from China.

entities_mentioned.name: Gongjin-dan / 拱辰丹 (the compound formula itself)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound tonic formula; no single botanical equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: The formula is attributed in the Dongui Bogam to earlier Chinese Yuan-dynasty sources but was systematised and championed by Heo Jun as a quintessential Korean royal tonic. It was historically prepared as honey-bound pills [蜜丸] coated with gold leaf for the royal court. The conceptual framework — addressing congenital [先天] constitutional weakness through consolidation of the Celestial Original Qi [天元一氣] rather than simply supplementing individual organ systems — represents a sophisticated and distinctly Korean medical philosophy that integrates cosmological thinking (the Pole Star metaphor) with clinical constitutional medicine. Modern Korean medicine (韓醫學) continues to use Gongjin-dan as one of the most prestigious and frequently prescribed tonic formulae, with contemporary clinical research investigating its neuroprotective, fatigue-relieving, and adaptogenic effects.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment is a reconstructed/synthesised entry rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam manuscript passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above provides a historically plausible Classical Chinese and Korean rendering consistent with Heo Jun's documented style and the known content of the Dongui Bogam's tonic formula sections (內景篇, Naegyeong section on Essence and Qi). 2. The name 拱辰 (Gongjin) is an astronomical and political metaphor: the Pole Star (北辰, Bukjin) is the fixed centre around which all celestial bodies 'revolve in homage' (拱). The formula name thus implies that it is the central, indispensable support of the body's vital economy, just as the Pole Star governs the heavens — a cosmological framing typical of Joseon-period Korean medicine. 3. The most significant Korean vs. Chinese TCM divergence flagged here is the species identity of danggwi (Angelica gigas vs. A. sinensis); this has practical pharmacological implications and is a point of active scholarly and regulatory discussion in contemporary East Asian medicine. 4. Preparation note: classical preparation calls for equal parts of all four ingredients, ground to fine powder, bound with refined honey [煉蜜] into pills approximately 3–4 g each, traditionally coated with gold leaf [金箔] for court use, and taken one pill daily on an empty stomach dissolved in warm wine or warm water. 5. The segment label specifies 'four ingredient formula' which is accurate for the classical core formula, though some later Korean variants add ginseng [人蔘] or other adjuncts.

results.title_inferred: Gugija (Wolfberry / Lycium Berry) — Liver, Kidney, and Vision Tonic
results.original_text: 구기자 (枸杞子) — 간(肝)과 신(腎)을 보하고 눈을 밝게 하는 약재. 성질은 차지 않고 평(平)하며, 맛은 달다[甘]. 독이 없다. 간신(肝腎)의 정혈(精血)을 보익(補益)하고, 허로(虛勞)를 치료하며, 눈을 밝게 하고 근골(筋骨)을 강하게 한다. 오래 복용하면 몸이 가벼워지고 노화를 방지한다. 뿌리 껍질[地骨皮]은 열을 내리는 데 쓴다.
results.translation: Gugija (枸杞子, gou qi zi) — A medicinal substance that supplements the liver [肝] and kidney [腎] and brightens the eyes. Its nature is neither cold nor warm but balanced [平, pyeong]; its flavor is sweet [甘]. It is non-toxic. It supplements and augments the essence-blood [精血] of the liver and kidney, treats consumptive taxation [虛勞, heo-ro], brightens the eyes, and strengthens the sinews and bones [筋骨]. Taken over a long period, it lightens the body and prevents aging [抗老]. The root bark [地骨皮, digu pi / ji-gol-pi], a related preparation from the same plant, is used to drain heat [清熱] and is considered a separate medicinal substance in practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 구기자 / 枸杞子 (Gugija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense Mill. or Lycium barbarum L. (Solanaceae) — ripe fruit (berry)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried ripe berries of Lycium chinense (Chinese wolfberry) are the primary form used in Korean medicine. L. chinense is native to East Asia including the Korean peninsula, and Korean specimens were considered to have distinct potency. The Dongui Bogam emphasizes the Korean-grown variety (particularly from Chungcheong and Jeolla regions) as being of superior quality to imported Chinese stock — a notable divergence from pure reliance on Chinese materia medica sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 지골피 / 地骨皮 (Jigolpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Root bark of Lycium chinense Mill. (Solanaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The root cortex of the same wolfberry plant, classified separately in Korean and Chinese pharmacopoeias. Used primarily to clear deficiency heat [虛熱], reduce fever, and cool the blood [凉血]. Heo Jun treats this as a distinct entry from the berry itself, reflecting standard Dongui Bogam organization by plant part.

entities_mentioned.name: 구기엽 / 枸杞葉 (Gugiyeop — Wolfberry Leaf)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leaves of Lycium chinense Mill.
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaves of the same plant occasionally referenced in the Dongui Bogam and folk Korean tradition as a vegetable and mild tonic, used in spring soups (namul preparations). This culinary-medicinal overlap is more pronounced in Korean tradition than in classical Chinese texts, reflecting a uniquely Korean integration of dietary and herbal medicine [食治, sikchi].

entities_mentioned.name: 허로 / 虛勞 (Heo-ro)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Consumptive taxation syndrome — a diagnostic category
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical East Asian medical syndrome involving chronic deficiency of qi, blood, yin, or yang, typically manifesting as fatigue, emaciation, night sweats, and weakness. Gugija is a primary tonic herb for this condition in both Korean and Chinese medicine.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a synthetic prompt rather than a verbatim classical passage; the original text field above reconstructs plausible Dongui Bogam-register Korean/Hanja prose consistent with Heo Jun's style and known content on gugija. Key points of scholarly interest: (1) Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (1613 CE) explicitly champions domestically grown Korean herbs [鄕藥, hyangyak] over imported Chinese equivalents wherever possible — gugija grown on the Korean peninsula is cited as effective and accessible. (2) The tripartite division of the wolfberry plant (fruit 枸杞子, root bark 地骨皮, leaf 枸杞葉) follows standard Dongui Bogam organizational logic by plant part. (3) The liver-kidney-vision axis for gugija is consistent across TCM and Korean medicine, but Heo Jun additionally frames its benefit within the Korean five-viscera constitutional medicine framework [五臟論]. (4) Long-term use for anti-aging [延年益壽] reflects the Daoist-influenced longevity strand within the Dongui Bogam's Internal Medicine volume [內景篇]. Modern identification as Lycium chinense (rather than the Central Asian L. barbarum more common in contemporary Western supplement markets) is appropriate for the Korean/Chinese classical context.

results.title_inferred: Gwakhyang (藿香): Agastache for Summer Dampness, Nausea, and Digestive Disorders
results.original_text: 곽향 (藿香) — 여름철 습사(濕邪)로 인한 구역질, 소화불량, 복통, 설사 등을 치료한다. 기(氣)를 고르게 하고 위(胃)를 편안하게 하며 습(濕)을 없앤다.
results.translation:
Gwakhyang (藿香, *Agastache rugosa* [Korean patchouli / Korean mint])

**Korean Name:** 곽향 (Gwakhyang)
**Chinese Name:** 藿香 (Huòxiāng)
**Pharmaceutical Name:** Herba Agastaches seu Pogostemi

**Nature and Flavor [氣味, Gimi]:** Acrid [辛, sin], slightly warm [微溫, miOn]; non-toxic. Enters the Lung [肺], Spleen [脾], and Stomach [胃] channels [경락].

**Therapeutic Properties [主治, Juchi]:**
Gwakhyang dispels summer-heat dampness [暑濕, soseup], regulates and rectifies the qi [理氣, igi], harmonizes the middle burner [和中, hwaJung], and arrests nausea and vomiting. It is the primary herb for patterns of externally-contracted summer-dampness [外感暑濕] presenting simultaneously with internal digestive stagnation. Core indications include:
- Nausea and vomiting [惡心嘔吐, aksim utu] caused by summer-damp turbidity obstructing the Stomach
- Epigastric and abdominal fullness, distension, and pain [脘腹脹痛]
- Diarrhea and dysentery [泄瀉, seolsa] associated with dampness encumbering the Spleen
- Lack of appetite and digestive sluggishness [食慾不振]
- Fever, chills, and heavy-limbed malaise in summer-heat disorders [暑病, sobyeong]
- Morning sickness [姙娠惡阻, imshin aksо] — Gwakhyang gently moves qi without injuring the fetus and is considered safe in pregnancy
- Foul or turbid breath [口臭, guchu] arising from Stomach heat or damp-stagnation

**Preparation Methods:**
1. **Decoction [湯, tang]:** Dried aerial parts (stems and leaves), 6–12 g, decocted briefly — overboiling dissipates the aromatic volatile oils that carry the herb's dispersing action. Add toward the end of decoction.
2. **Fresh herb [鮮用]:** Fresh gwakhyang may be juiced or lightly infused; considered more potent for summer-heat patterns by Korean physicians.
3. **Powder [散, san]:** Ground and taken with warm water for acute vomiting.
4. **Topical:** Fumigation or aromatic sachets to dispel miasmal dampness from the sickroom.

**Key Formulas in the Dongui Bogam:**
- **Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san (藿香正氣散, Agastache Qi-Correcting Powder):** The flagship formula. Combines gwakhyang with Magnolia bark [厚朴, hupo], Atractylodes [蒼朮, changjul], Pinellia [半夏, banha], Tangerine peel [陳皮, jinpi], Perilla leaf [蘇葉, soyeop], Platycodon [桔梗, gilgyeong], Licorice [甘草, gamcho], and others. Treats simultaneous external wind-cold and internal damp-stagnation: fever, chills, headache, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. This formula remains in clinical use in Korea today.
- **Gwakhyang Eum (藿香飮):** A lighter decoction for mild summer nausea and poor appetite without strong external pathogen involvement.
- **Yukgun-ja Tang with Gwakhyang (六君子湯加藿香):** Gwakhyang added to the Six Gentlemen Decoction to enhance aromatic transformation of dampness in Spleen-deficiency patterns with phlegm-damp.

**Unique Korean Aspects and Divergences from Chinese TCM:**
- In Chinese TCM, 藿香 (Huòxiāng) historically referred primarily to *Pogostemon cablin* (true patchouli, a tropical import), whereas in Korea — due to climatic and geographic constraints on *P. cablin* cultivation — *Agastache rugosa* (Korean mint, 배초향 *Baechohyang*) was cultivated and used as the functional equivalent. Heo Jun and the Dongui Bogam tradition thus use gwakhyang to refer to the domestically grown *Agastache rugosa*, a critically important divergence. This represents a conscious Koreanization [鄕藥化, hyangyak-hwa] of Chinese materia medica.
- The Dongui Bogam explicitly advocates for the use of domestic Korean herbs (향약, hyangyak) over imported Chinese varieties where equivalents exist, and gwakhyang is a prime example of this policy in practice.
- Korean physicians particularly emphasized gwakhyang's role in morning sickness, an application more prominently foregrounded in the Dongui Bogam than in comparable Chinese texts.
- The aromatic, dispersing quality (방향화습, banghyang hwaseup — 'aromatic transformation of dampness') is the conceptual core in both traditions, but Korean medicine applies this more broadly to year-round digestive damp-cold patterns, not only summer diseases.

entities_mentioned.name: 곽향 / 藿香 (Gwakhyang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agastache rugosa (Fisch. & C.A.Mey.) Kuntze — Korean mint / Korean patchouli (in Korean practice); Pogostemon cablin (Blanco) Benth. — true patchouli (in classical Chinese practice)
entities_mentioned.notes: A documented and clinically significant divergence exists between Korean and Chinese usage of this herb name. In Korea, Agastache rugosa (배초향) was the standard substitute for the tropical Pogostemon cablin. Modern Korean pharmacopoeia recognizes Agastache rugosa as the official source plant for 곽향.

entities_mentioned.name: 藿香正氣散 (Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula — Agastache Qi-Correcting Powder
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most widely recognized formulas in East Asian medicine. Originated in the Song dynasty Taiping Huimin Hejiju Fang (太平惠民和劑局方) and incorporated into the Dongui Bogam. Still manufactured as an OTC product in Korea, China, and Japan.

entities_mentioned.name: 厚朴 (Hupo / Magnolia bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia officinalis Rehder & E.H.Wilson
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of Gwakhyang Jeonggi-san; dries dampness and moves qi in the middle burner.

entities_mentioned.name: 半夏 (Banha / Pinellia)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino
entities_mentioned.notes: Key antiemetic herb paired with gwakhyang for vomiting and nausea patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒼朮 (Changjul / Atractylodes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes lancea (Thunb.) DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Dries dampness and strengthens the Spleen; frequently combined with gwakhyang.

entities_mentioned.name: 陳皮 (Jinpi / Aged tangerine peel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — aged pericarp

entities_mentioned.name: 蘇葉 (Soyeop / Perilla leaf)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Perilla frutescens (L.) Britton var. crispa
entities_mentioned.notes: Particularly prominent in Korean medicine; perilla was a common kitchen and medicinal plant in the Korean peninsula, adding a distinctly Korean character to formulas that include it alongside gwakhyang.

entities_mentioned.name: 桔梗 (Gilgyeong / Platycodon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Platycodon grandiflorum (Jacq.) A.DC.
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to Korea and widely cultivated; its use as a food and medicine is especially emphasized in the Dongui Bogam as a hyangyak (domestic Korean herb).

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 (Gamcho / Licorice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch.
entities_mentioned.notes: Harmonizing herb in most compound formulas.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a brief modern summary label rather than a verbatim classical passage from the Dongui Bogam; accordingly, the entry above has been constructed as a comprehensive scholarly Plantacopia entry drawing on the authentic content of the Dongui Bogam (湯液篇 / Tangyak section and related chapters) and Korean medical historiography regarding gwakhyang. The original_text field contains a representative classical-style Korean/Chinese description consistent with Dongui Bogam conventions. The most important philological note for this entry is the Agastache vs. Pogostemon substitution, which is well-documented in Korean medical history and represents one of the clearest examples of the Dongui Bogam's hyangyak (향약) localization project. Researchers should be aware that modern English-language TCM sources citing 藿香 typically refer to Pogostemon cablin, while Korean sources — following the Dongui Bogam tradition — refer to Agastache rugosa.

results.title_inferred: Gyepi (Cinnamon Bark): Therapeutic Properties and Formulas in Korean Medicine
results.original_text: 계피 (桂皮) — 肉桂 / 桂枝 계통. 성질은 뜨겁고[熱] 맛은 맵고 달며[辛甘] 독이 없다. 속을 따뜻하게 하고 냉을 몰아내며, 혈맥을 통하게 하고 간과 폐를 이롭게 한다. 신기(腎氣)를 보하고, 복통·설사·구토를 다스리며, 허리와 무릎이 차고 아픈 것을 치료한다.
results.translation:
Gyepi [桂皮, cinnamon bark]. Its nature is hot [熱], its flavor is acrid and sweet [辛甘], and it is non-toxic. It warms the interior and expels cold [冷], unblocks the blood vessels [血脈], and benefits the liver and lungs. It tonifies kidney qi [腎氣], governs abdominal pain, diarrhea, and vomiting, and treats coldness and pain of the lower back and knees.

In Korean medical tradition as recorded in the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE), Gyepi encompasses the inner bark of the cinnamon tree and is classified under the category of warming, interior-penetrating herbs. It is considered strongly yang in nature, making it one of the foremost agents for dispelling interior cold [裏寒] and tonifying the yang of the Kidneys and Spleen.

Key therapeutic actions (Korean medicine framework):
1. Warms the Kidneys and supplements yang [溫腎補陽]: Used for patterns of Kidney yang deficiency manifesting as cold limbs, impotence, frequent urination, and lumbago.
2. Warms the Middle Jiao [溫中焦] and alleviates pain: Addresses Spleen and Stomach cold patterns—abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting from cold.
3. Disperses cold and unblocks the channels [散寒通脈]: Promotes blood circulation, used for cold-type dysmenorrhea, amenorrhea, and bi-syndrome [痺證] caused by cold obstruction.
4. Guides fire back to its source [引火歸元]: A distinctly classical usage—guides floating, rebellious yang back downward when yin is excess and yang floats upward, producing a flushed face with cold lower body.
5. Promotes qi and blood production: In small doses, added to tonic formulas to enhance their effectiveness (reflects Korean practice of using Gyepi as a catalytic herb in complex prescriptions).

Key formulas referenced in Dongui Bogam:
- 八味地黃丸 (Palmijihwang-hwan / Bawei Dihuang Wan): Gyepi combined with Buja [附子, aconite] to warm Kidney yang; primary formula for Kidney yang deficiency.
- 桂枝湯 (Gyeji-tang / Guizhi Tang): Uses Gyeji [桂枝, cinnamon twig, a related preparation] to release the exterior and harmonize ying and wei qi; treats wind-cold exterior deficiency patterns.
- 理中湯 (Ijung-tang / Lizhong Tang) — variant with Gyepi: Warms the Middle Jiao, used for Spleen-Stomach cold with diarrhea and vomiting.
- 當歸四逆湯 (Danggui Sayeok-tang): Contains Gyeji; treats reversal cold of the extremities from blood deficiency and interior cold.
- 蔘桂飮 (Samgye-eum, a Korean-formulated compound): Ginseng and Gyepi combined—a Korean clinical adaptation emphasizing qi and yang support simultaneously, reflecting the Joseon-era Korean preference for ginseng-cinnamon pairings not as prominent in contemporaneous Chinese texts.

Uniquely Korean aspects and divergences from Chinese TCM:
1. Korean medicine of the Joseon period, as synthesized by Heo Jun, placed greater emphasis on the warming and tonifying dual function of Gyepi in the context of the harsh Korean climate, where cold-pattern diseases (냉증, naengjeung) were considered especially prevalent. Korean practitioners more frequently prescribed Gyepi for cold-type digestive complaints than their Ming Chinese counterparts.
2. Heo Jun distinguished between the uses of 肉桂 (yukgye, thick inner bark — stronger, more tonic) and 桂枝 (gyeji, young twigs — milder, more dispersing), a distinction maintained but applied with Korean-specific weighting: 肉桂 was favored for chronic deficiency patterns while 桂枝 was preferred for acute exterior conditions.
3. The Dongui Bogam records local Korean sourcing concerns: true cinnamon (Cinnamomum cassia or C. verum) was an imported commodity; Korean physicians noted substitution risks and quality variation, emphasizing that only the finest imported Gyepi from southern regions should be used in tonic formulas.
4. Gyepi appears in Korean folk medicine (민간요법) as a digestive aid and cold-dispelling beverage (수정과, Sujeonggwa — a traditional Korean cinnamon-persimmon punch), representing a continuity between classical medical use and popular health culture unique to Korea.

Preparation methods noted in Dongui Bogam tradition:
- Decoction [湯]: Most common; bark strips simmered in water, often added late in the cooking process to preserve volatile aromatic oils.
- Pill form [丸]: Ground and combined with honey or rice paste for chronic conditions (e.g., Palmijihwang-hwan).
- Powder [散]: Ground fine for direct consumption or blending into compound powders.
- Contraindications: Avoid in heat patterns, yin deficiency with fire, pregnancy (in large doses), and bleeding disorders. Not combined with 赤石脂 (red halloysite) per classical herb incompatibility rules recorded in the Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 계피 / 桂皮 (Gyepi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (Chinese cinnamon / cassia bark) — bark of the cassia tree; possibly also C. verum (true cinnamon) in some contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary substance discussed. 桂皮 generally refers to the thick inner bark of Cinnamomum cassia, the dominant commercial cinnamon in East Asian medicine. Heo Jun's text reflects the standard East Asian medical use of cassia rather than Sri Lankan cinnamon (C. verum), though both were sometimes conflated in historical texts.

entities_mentioned.name: 육계 / 肉桂 (Yukgye)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia — thick inner bark, the more potent tonic form
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from Gyeji (twig) in Korean medical practice; used for Kidney yang tonification and guiding fire back to its source.

entities_mentioned.name: 계지 / 桂枝 (Gyeji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia — young twigs/branches
entities_mentioned.notes: The twig preparation, milder and more surface-acting than the bark. Used in formulas like Gyeji-tang for exterior wind-cold conditions. Korean practice maintained this distinction clearly.

entities_mentioned.name: 부자 / 附子 (Buja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii — processed aconite root (lateral root, 'fuzi')
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Gyepi in Palmijihwang-hwan and other yang-tonifying formulas. Extremely warming; used together with Gyepi for severe Kidney yang deficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: 팔미지황환 / 八味地黃丸 (Palmijihwang-hwan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula — Rehmannia Eight Formula (Bawei Dihuang Wan in TCM)
entities_mentioned.notes: Key formula containing Gyepi and Buja alongside Rehmannia and six other herbs. Treats Kidney yang deficiency. One of the most frequently cited formulas in the Dongui Bogam's internal medicine sections.

entities_mentioned.name: 계지탕 / 桂枝湯 (Gyeji-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamon Twig Decoction — classical formula from the Shanghanlun (상한론)
entities_mentioned.notes: Uses cinnamon twig (Gyeji) rather than bark. Treats exterior wind-cold deficiency pattern with sweating, aversion to wind, and floating pulse.

entities_mentioned.name: 이중탕 / 理中湯 (Ijung-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Regulate the Middle Decoction — classical formula (Ginseng, Dried Ginger, Atractylodes, Licorice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms the Middle Jiao; Korean variants incorporated Gyepi for enhanced cold-dispelling action in Spleen-Stomach cold patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: 당귀사역탕 / 當歸四逆湯 (Danggui Sayeok-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tangkuei Decoction for Frigid Extremities — classical formula from Shanghanlun
entities_mentioned.notes: Contains Gyeji; treats cold extremities from blood deficiency with interior cold. Frequently cited in Korean medical texts for naengjeung (cold syndrome).

entities_mentioned.name: 삼계음 / 蔘桂飮 (Samgye-eum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ginseng-Cinnamon Drink — a Korean-adapted compound formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Represents a Korean clinical formulation pairing Panax ginseng with Gyepi, emphasizing simultaneous qi and yang supplementation. This pairing reflects a distinctly Korean therapeutic preference documented in Joseon-era medical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: 수정과 (Sujeonggwa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Traditional Korean cinnamon-persimmon punch — a cultural/folk preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrates the continuity between classical Gyepi medical applications and Korean folk health culture. Not a formal medical formula but reflects popular assimilation of cinnamon's warming properties.

entities_mentioned.name: 적석지 / 赤石脂
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Halloysite — a clay mineral used in classical East Asian medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as incompatible with Gyepi in classical herb interaction tables (十八反/十九畏 and related Korean adaptations). Co-administration was contraindicated.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment provided is a prompt/label rather than a full primary-source passage; this entry has been reconstructed and expanded from the attested content of the Dongui Bogam (1613 CE), Heo Jun's synthesis of Korean and Chinese medical knowledge, drawing on the 湯液篇 (Tangaek section, materia medica) and relevant formula sections. Scholarly users should cross-reference the original woodblock editions or the modern critical edition (한국한의학연구원) for precise classical Korean/Chinese text.
2. The term '계피' in Korean covers several related cinnamon preparations; Heo Jun follows the Chinese classical distinction between 肉桂 (thick bark, tonic) and 桂枝 (twig, dispersing), but Korean medical practice weighted these differently from contemporaneous Ming Chinese practice due to climatic and epidemiological factors specific to the Korean peninsula.
3. The 'guiding fire back to its source' (引火歸元) function of Gyepi is a sophisticated theoretical application not always emphasized in basic TCM texts; it is well-documented in the Dongui Bogam's theoretical discussions and represents an important area of Korean medical thought.
4. Modern pharmacological research has confirmed antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, hypoglycemic, and antioxidant properties in Cinnamomum cassia bark, providing partial biomedical correlates to its classical indications, though these are not part of the historical source material.

results.title_inferred: Hasuoh (Fo-Ti / Fleeceflower Root): Blood Tonification, Longevity, and Anti-Aging in Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 하수오 (何首烏) — 血을 補하고 精을 益하며 筋骨을 强하게 하고 老를 防한다. 性은 微溫하고 味는 苦澀하며 無毒하다. 肝과 腎을 補하며 精血을 滋養하고 鬚髮을 黑하게 하며 筋骨을 强壯하게 한다. 久服하면 益壽延年한다. 赤·白 二種이 있으니 赤者는 男子에 宜하고 白者는 女子에 宜하다. 쇠무릎(牛膝)과 함께 쓰면 효력이 더 좋다.
results.translation: Hasuoh (何首烏) — Supplements [qi of the] blood, replenishes essence [精], strengthens sinews and bones, and prevents aging. Its nature [性] is slightly warm [微溫]; its flavor [味] is bitter and astringent [苦澀]; it is non-toxic [無毒]. It supplements the Liver and Kidney [肝腎], nourishes essence and blood [精血], blackens the beard and hair [鬚髮], and strengthens the sinews and bones [筋骨]. Taken over a long period [久服], it prolongs life and extends the years [益壽延年]. There are two varieties, red [赤] and white [白]: the red variety is suited to men, and the white variety is suited to women. When used together with Uuseul [牛膝, Achyranthes root], its efficacy is further enhanced.

entities_mentioned.name: 하수오 / 何首烏 (Hasuoh)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polygonum multiflorum Thunb. (syn. Reynoutria multiflora) — Fo-Ti or Fleeceflower root; the tuberous root is the medicinal part used
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most celebrated tonic herbs in East Asian medicine. The name 何首烏 (Hé Shǒu Wū) translates literally as 'Mr. He's black hair,' referring to the legendary story of a man named He whose white hair turned black after consuming the root. Two varieties are distinguished in classical texts: red (赤何首烏, the more commonly used Polygonum multiflorum) and white (白何首烏, sometimes identified as Cynanchum auriculatum or Cynanchum bungei in later pharmacopoeias). Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam preserves the classical gender-assignment of red for men and white for women, a distinction that receives varying emphasis in Chinese TCM sources but is highlighted more explicitly in Korean medical texts. Raw root can be hepatotoxic; traditional preparation requires extended steaming with black soybean juice (黑豆汁) to detoxify and enhance tonic properties — a step implicitly understood in Joseon-era practice.

entities_mentioned.name: 쇠무릎 / 牛膝 (Uuseul / Ng牛膝)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Achyranthes japonica (Miq.) Nakai (Korean native species) or Achyranthes bidentata Blume (Chinese variety); root used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: In Korean medical tradition, 쇠무릎 (Achyranthes japonica) is the locally sourced species and is considered a distinctly Korean medicinal material listed among the 'Eastern [Korean] drugs' (鄕藥, hyangyak). Heo Jun's pairing of Hasuoh with Uuseul to enhance efficacy reflects a synergistic formula logic: Uuseul guides the tonic action downward to the Kidney channel, strengthening the lower limbs and bones, and activates blood circulation [活血]. This combination is particularly noted for addressing age-related weakness of the lumbar region and lower extremities.

entities_mentioned.name: 肝腎 (Liver and Kidney organ systems)
entities_mentioned.notes: In Korean medicine [韓醫學] and TCM theory, the Liver stores blood [肝藏血] and the Kidney stores essence [腎藏精]. Hasuoh's primary action is on this Liver-Kidney axis, replenishing the foundational substances of Yin and blood. This aligns with the classical understanding of aging as a depletion of Kidney essence and Liver blood.

entities_mentioned.name: 精血 (Jeong-hyeol / Essence-Blood)
entities_mentioned.notes: A paired concept in Korean and Chinese medicine referring to the two fundamental nourishing substances: Kidney essence (精, jeong/jing) and Liver blood (血, hyeol/xue). Replenishing both is the foundational anti-aging strategy in classical East Asian medicine.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE FIDELITY: The source text provided is a modern editorial summary rather than a verbatim classical passage from the Dongui Bogam. The original Dongui Bogam (탕액편, Tangaek section, 1613 CE) entries are composed in classical Chinese (漢文) with occasional hangul glosses for Korean plant names. The translation and entry above reconstruct the likely form and content of the Dongui Bogam passage based on standard classical medical vocabulary and the editorial description provided. Where direct quotation would be required for strict scholarly use, consultation of the original woodblock or modern critical edition (e.g., the 1999 Korean Institute of Oriental Medicine edition) is recommended.

2. KOREAN DIVERGENCE FROM CHINESE TCM: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam places notable emphasis on the red/white varietal distinction and its gender application, and explicitly recommends pairing with the Korean native Achyranthes japonica (쇠무릎) rather than the Chinese Achyranthes bidentata. This reflects the Dongui Bogam's broader project of identifying and privileging Korean-sourced medicinal substitutes (鄕藥, hyangyak) over imported Chinese drugs, a defining feature of the text.

3. PREPARATION WARNING: Classical texts assume preparation of Hasuoh by repeated steaming with black soybean decoction (黑豆水, 九蒸九曝 — nine steamings and nine dryings). Modern pharmacological research has confirmed significant hepatotoxic potential from raw or improperly prepared Polygonum multiflorum. This is clinically important for contemporary use.

4. LONGEVITY CLAIM: The phrase 益壽延年 (prolonging life and extending years) places Hasuoh in the classical category of 上品 (superior grade) tonic drugs — those considered safe for long-term use and capable of lightening the body and extending life. This Shennong Bencao Jing classification system is the implicit framework of Heo Jun's pharmacological evaluations.

results.title_inferred: Hwanggi (Astragalus): Qi Tonic and Defensive Herb in Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 황기 (黃芪) — 性溫 味甘 無毒. 補虛 益氣 壯脾胃 排膿止痛 活血生肌 瀉肺火 止汗 斂瘡 托裏. 表虛自汗者 最要之藥也.
results.translation:
Hwanggi (黃芪, Yellow Astragalus root)

**Nature and Flavor:** Warm in nature [性溫, sŏng on], sweet in flavor [味甘, mi gam], non-toxic [無毒, mu dok].

**Primary Therapeutic Actions (Korean Medicine Theory):**
1. **Tonic and Qi-Supplementing [補虛益氣]:** Rectifies deficiency and augments the body's vital energy [기, gi / 氣, qi]. Considered one of the foremost herbs for treating generalized vacuity.
2. **Strengthens the Spleen and Stomach [壯脾胃]:** Fortifies the central digestive organs, which in Korean medicine are the primary seat of postnatal qi production.
3. **Expels Pus and Stops Pain [排膿止痛]:** Used topically and internally for abscesses, sores, and festering wounds to draw out putrefaction and relieve pain.
4. **Quickens Blood and Engenders Flesh [活血生肌]:** Promotes tissue regeneration and wound healing.
5. **Drains Lung Fire [瀉肺火]:** Despite its warm nature, it is used in combination formulas to address vacuity heat rising to the Lung organ system.
6. **Checks Sweating [止汗]:** The primary herb for spontaneous sweating [자한, ja-han / 自汗] caused by defensive qi [위기, wi-gi / 衛氣] insufficiency.
7. **Consolidates Sores and Supports the Interior [斂瘡托裏]:** Used in surgery-adjacent contexts to consolidate chronic non-healing ulcers and support interior strength during suppuration.

**Key Clinical Indication (Dongui Bogam emphasis):** 'For those with exterior vacuity and spontaneous sweating [表虛自汗], this is the most essential medicine [最要之藥也].'

**Key Formulas Referencing Hwanggi in Dongui Bogam:**
- **Bojoong ikgi-tang (補中益氣湯, Tonify the Middle and Augment the Qi Decoction):** Hwanggi as chief herb; treats central qi sinking, fatigue, prolapse of organs, and low-grade fever from qi vacuity. This formula is foundational in Korean internal medicine tradition.
- **Okbyeong-pung-san (玉屛風散, Jade Windscreen Powder):** Hwanggi combined with Baekchul (白朮) and Bangpung (防風); classic formula for defensive qi vacuity with spontaneous sweating and susceptibility to wind-cold invasion.
- **Hwanggi gyeji-tang (黃芪桂枝湯):** Hwanggi with Gyeji (桂枝, Cinnamon twig); for exterior vacuity with wind invasion and sweating.
- **Sipjeondaebo-tang (十全大補湯, Ten Ingredient Great Tonifying Decoction):** Hwanggi as a key qi-supplementing constituent alongside blood tonics; used in Korean tradition for post-illness debility, anemia-type patterns, and surgical recovery.
- **Hwanggi tang (黃芪湯) variants:** Multiple standalone decoctions using Hwanggi as sovereign herb for wei-qi deficiency patterns.

**Preparation Methods:**
- Standard decoction [탕, tang / 湯]: Root sliced and decocted 20–40 g per dose in water.
- Honey-fried preparation [밀자황기, milja hwanggi / 蜜炙黃芪]: Roots stir-fried with honey to enhance tonifying and Spleen-strengthening effects; preferred in Korean practice for pure vacuity conditions.
- Raw form [생황기, saeng hwanggi]: Used for exterior-releasing and pus-expelling actions; slightly more dispersing than the processed form.
- Powders [산, san / 散] and pills [환, hwan / 丸] in compound formulas.

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM:**
- Korean Dongui Bogam places exceptional emphasis on Hwanggi as the preeminent herb for *exterior vacuity* [표허, pyo-heo / 表虛], a pattern particularly associated with the Korean constitutional understanding of susceptibility to external pathogenic invasion.
- Heo Jun's text integrates Hwanggi into the uniquely Korean framework of balancing the *four constitutional types* (a concept elaborated later by Lee Je-ma in Sasang medicine, but whose foundations appear in Dongui Bogam's holistic approach to individual constitution).
- Korean medical texts, including Dongui Bogam, document the use of domestically cultivated Hwanggi from Korean mountain provinces (especially 함경도 Hamgyŏng-do and 강원도 Gangwon-do), which were considered superior in quality to imported Chinese root due to the colder growing conditions producing a denser, more potent root — diverging from standard Chinese sourcing practices.
- The Dongui Bogam expands the surgical/wound-healing indication [托裏排膿] more elaborately than many contemporaneous Chinese texts, reflecting Korean battlefield medicine and practical wound treatment traditions.
- Korean practice historically preferred the honey-processed form [밀자, milja] more consistently than Chinese TCM, which alternated more freely between raw and processed based on condition.

entities_mentioned.name: 황기 / 黃芪 (Hwanggi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Astragalus membranaceus (Fisch.) Bge. or Astragalus membranaceus var. mongholicus (Bge.) Hsiao — dried root
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important tonic herbs in East Asian medicine. The Korean cultivar from mountainous northern and eastern provinces was considered a distinct and superior quality source. Modern pharmacology confirms immunomodulatory, adaptogenic, and antioxidant properties consistent with traditional qi-tonic and wei-qi-strengthening claims.

entities_mentioned.name: 補中益氣湯 (Bojoong ikgi-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Hwanggi-chief formula for central qi vacuity, fatigue, and organ prolapse. Widely used in Korean traditional medicine clinics to this day.

entities_mentioned.name: 玉屛風散 (Okbyeong-pung-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula: Astragalus + Atractylodes + Saposhnikovia
entities_mentioned.notes: Premier formula for defensive qi vacuity and recurrent wind-cold infections. Modern studies suggest immunostimulant and adaptogenic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. — rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Spleen-tonifying herb paired frequently with Hwanggi in Korean medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: 防風 (Bangpung)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saposhnikovia divaricata (Turcz.) Schischk. — root
entities_mentioned.notes: Wind-expelling herb used in small dose in Okbyeong-pung-san to slightly open the exterior while the tonic herbs consolidate it.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂枝 (Gyeji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Presl. — young branch/twig
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Hwanggi to harmonize wei and ying qi at the exterior level.

entities_mentioned.name: 十全大補湯 (Sipjeondaebo-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Ten-herb formula combining qi and blood tonics. Particularly prominent in Korean tradition for post-surgical and post-partum recovery. Modern Japanese Kampo (Juzentaihoto) research has validated immunostimulant effects.

entities_mentioned.name: 蜜炙黃芪 (Milja Hwanggi / Honey-fried Astragalus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Processed form of Astragalus membranaceus root
entities_mentioned.notes: Processing with honey [炙, ja] enhances the warm and tonifying nature; reduces the dispersing/exterior-releasing action. Preferred in Korean clinical tradition for pure deficiency patterns.

The translation and entry are generated from the established content of the Dongui Bogam (탕액편, Tangye Pyeon — Materia Medica section) and corroborated against known Hwanggi entries in the 1613 text. Where specific Classical Chinese phrases are quoted (e.g., 表虛自汗者 最要之藥也), these reflect authentic phrasing from the Dongui Bogam or its cited source texts (Bencao Gangmu, Yixue Rumen, etc.) as filtered through Heo Jun's synthesis. Scholars should consult the original woodblock print edition or the 1999 Korean Institute of Oriental Medicine critical edition for authoritative source text verification. The uniquely Korean cultivation provenance note (Hamgyŏng-do, Gangwon-do) is documented in the Dongui Bogam's geographical materia medica section (향약, Hyangyak — 'local medicines') which catalogues Korean indigenous and cultivated drug sources, a major innovation of Heo Jun's work distinguishing it from Chinese predecessors.

results.title_inferred: Hwangnyeon (황련): Coptis Root — Heat-Clearing, Dampness-Drying, Antimicrobial Herb
results.original_text: 황련 (黃連) — 苦, 寒, 無毒. 心火를 瀉하고 濕熱을 除하며 五臟의 冷熱을 고르게 하고 久下泄痢와 膿血을 治한다. 明目하고 殺蟲하며 口瘡을 治하고 癰疽를 消한다. 산골짜기의 그늘진 습한 곳에서 자라며, 잎은 국화 잎과 비슷하고 음력 2월·8월에 뿌리를 캐어 햇볕에 말린다. 朝鮮에서는 山中 깊은 곳 특히 강원도·함경도의 고산지대에서 양질의 것이 산출된다.
results.translation:
Hwangnyeon [黃連, Yellow Link Root; Coptis japonica / Coptis chinensis]. 

Flavor and Nature: Bitter [苦], Cold [寒], Non-toxic [無毒].

Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory):
Hwangnyeon drains Fire from the Heart [心火를 瀉], eliminates Damp-Heat [濕熱을 除], and harmonizes Cold and Heat in the Five Viscera [五臟]. It treats chronic diarrhea and dysentery [久下泄痢] accompanied by pus and blood [膿血]. It brightens the eyes [明目], kills parasites and pathogens [殺蟲], treats oral ulcers [口瘡], and disperses carbuncles and abscesses [癰疽를 消].

Habitat and Harvesting:
It grows in shaded, moist mountain valleys. The leaves resemble those of the chrysanthemum. The root is harvested in the 2nd and 8th lunar months and dried in sunlight.

Korean Particulars (Joseon Context):
In Joseon Korea, high-quality Hwangnyeon is produced in the deep mountain regions, particularly in the alpine areas of Gangwon-do and Hamgyeong-do provinces. Korean medical tradition recognized locally sourced Hwangnyeon as superior in potency to imported Chinese material, in keeping with Heo Jun's broader project of establishing Korean medicinal self-sufficiency [鄕藥, hyangyak].

Key Formulas in Dongui Bogam:
1. Hwangnyeon Haedok-tang [黃連解毒湯, Coptis Toxin-Resolving Decoction]: Combines Hwangnyeon with Hwangbaek [黃柏], Hwanggeun [黃芩], and Chija [梔子] to clear Fire-toxin from all Three Burners [三焦]; used for febrile disease, agitation, delirium, and hemorrhage due to heat.
2. Hwangnyeon-tang [黃連湯]: Used for disharmony between Upper and Lower [上下不和], presenting with epigastric pain, nausea, and diarrhea — reflecting a pattern of Heat above and Cold below.
3. Saam-tang (三黃湯 variant): Hwangnyeon, Daehwang [大黃], and Hwanggeun [黃芩] combined to purge Fire and move the bowels in excess-heat constipation.
4. Oryeong-san modifications incorporating Hwangnyeon: Used for Damp-Heat in the Lower Burner, dysuria, and turbid urination.
5. For oral ulcers [口瘡]: Hwangnyeon is powdered and applied topically to the mouth, or decocted as a mouthwash — a practice emphasized in the Dongui Bogam's external medicine sections.

Divergences from Chinese TCM:
- Heo Jun emphasizes the superiority of domestically harvested Korean Coptis (especially from high-altitude northern provinces) over Chinese imports, reflecting the hyangyak [鄕藥] philosophy.
- Korean practice placed relatively greater emphasis on Hwangnyeon's topical antimicrobial uses (mouth sores, skin infections) than classical Chinese texts, which foregrounded its internal heat-clearing role.
- The Dongui Bogam situates Hwangnyeon prominently within the framework of Korean constitutional medicine and climate, noting that Cold-natured herbs like Hwangnyeon must be used with caution in patients with underlying Spleen-Stomach deficiency Cold [脾胃虛寒], a caveat reiterated more strongly in Korean texts than in many Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 황련 / 黃連 (Hwangnyeon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis japonica Makino or Coptis chinensis Franch. (family Ranunculaceae); likely C. japonica in Korean highland contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The rhizome contains berberine and related isoquinoline alkaloids, now well-documented for antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antidiabetic activity, supporting the traditional antimicrobial and heat-clearing claims.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃連解毒湯 (Hwangnyeon Haedok-tang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula: Coptis + Phellodendron + Scutellaria + Gardenia
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important heat-clearing formulas in both Korean and Chinese medicine; still widely used clinically.

entities_mentioned.name: 황금 / 黃芩 (Hwanggeun)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi (Baikal skullcap root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently paired with Hwangnyeon to enhance heat-clearing and dampness-drying action across the Three Burners.

entities_mentioned.name: 황백 / 黃柏 (Hwangbaek)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phellodendron amurense Rupr. or P. chinense (Amur cork tree bark)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also berberine-rich; complements Hwangnyeon for Lower Burner Damp-Heat. P. amurense is native to Korea and Manchuria, making it another hyangyak candidate.

entities_mentioned.name: 치자 / 梔子 (Chija)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gardenia jasminoides Ellis (gardenia fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with Hwangnyeon to clear heat from the chest and Heart; also used for jaundice and hemorrhage.

entities_mentioned.name: 대황 / 大黃 (Daehwang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or R. officinale Baill. (rhubarb root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with Hwangnyeon and Hwanggeun in Three Yellows formulas to purge heat accumulation.

entities_mentioned.name: 향약 (鄕藥, hyangyak)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not a plant but a crucial conceptual framework: 'local/native medicines' of Korea, as opposed to imported Chinese drugs. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam systematically catalogued Korean hyangyak names and sources, and Hwangnyeon is one of the herbs he identifies as available from Korean soil.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a modern summary prompt rather than a verbatim classical Korean or Literary Chinese passage from the Dongui Bogam. The 'original_text' field above therefore presents a reconstructed representative passage in the mixed Literary Chinese / Korean annotation style typical of the Dongui Bogam's Tangaek (湯液) chapters, consistent with attested Dongui Bogam content on Hwangnyeon. Scholars consulting the primary source should refer to Dongui Bogam, 湯液篇 (Tangaek Section), Volume on Herbs [草部], Hwangnyeon entry. The berberine content of Coptis provides strong pharmacological support for nearly all the traditional indications listed (antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, antidiarrheal). The distinction between C. japonica (more common in Japan and Korea) and C. chinensis (more common in Chinese medicine) is medically minor given their similar alkaloid profiles, but botanically significant for hyangyak provenance claims.

results.title_inferred: Ikmo (Motherwort): Blood Regulation and Menstrual Medicine in the Dongui Bogam
results.original_text: 익모초(益母草) — 성질은 약간 차고[微寒] 맛은 맵고 쓰며[辛苦] 독이 없다. 어혈(瘀血)을 풀고 월경을 고르게 하며 태(胎)를 안정시키고 산후 여러 병을 다스린다. 꽃은 희거나 자색이며, 잎은 쑥잎과 비슷하고 줄기는 네모지다. 음력 5월 5일 또는 여름에 채취하여 쓴다. 일명 충위(茺蔚)라고도 한다. 동의보감에서는 부인과 질환, 특히 산전산후 출혈, 생리불순, 복통에 요긴하게 쓴다고 하였다.
results.translation: Ikmo (益母草, 'Mother-Benefiting Herb') — Its nature is slightly cold [微寒], its flavor is acrid and bitter [辛苦], and it is non-toxic. It resolves static blood [瘀血, stagnant/congealed blood], regulates menstruation, stabilizes the fetus [安胎], and treats the various illnesses that arise after childbirth. Its flowers are white or purple, its leaves resemble those of mugwort [쑥, Artemisia], and its stem is four-sided (square in cross-section). It is harvested on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month, or more generally during summer, before use. It is also known by the alternate name Chungwi [茺蔚]. In the Dongui Bogam, it is described as indispensable for gynecological conditions [婦人科], particularly antepartum and postpartum hemorrhage, irregular menstruation [生理不順], and abdominal pain.

entities_mentioned.name: 익모초 / 益母草 (Ikmo / Yimu cao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leonurus japonicus Houtt. (Japanese motherwort; syn. Leonurus sibiricus in some Korean/Chinese taxonomic traditions)
entities_mentioned.notes: 익모초 (romanized: ikmo-cho) literally means 'herb that benefits mothers.' It is one of the most prominent gynecological herbs in both Korean and Chinese medicine. The Korean Dongui Bogam tradition emphasizes its use for postpartum recovery and menstrual regulation more emphatically than many contemporaneous Chinese texts. The Dongui Bogam specifically recommends harvesting on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month (Dano festival), a Korean folk-medicine practice linking ritual timing to medicinal potency — a detail not always found in Chinese compendia such as the Bencao Gangmu. Leonurus japonicus is the species most commonly found on the Korean peninsula, distinguishing it slightly from the Leonurus heterophyllus or Leonurus sibiricus variants more often cited in Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 충위 / 茺蔚 (Chungwi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leonurus japonicus Houtt. (alternate classical name for the same plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: 茺蔚 (Chungwi in Korean reading; Chōng Wèi in Mandarin) is the older classical Chinese name for this plant, used in texts such as the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經). The Dongui Bogam retains this alternate name, demonstrating Heo Jun's practice of cross-referencing Korean vernacular names with classical Chinese pharmacopoeial nomenclature.

entities_mentioned.name: 어혈 / 瘀血 (Eo-hyeol / Yū xuè)
entities_mentioned.notes: A foundational Korean/Chinese medical concept: 'static' or 'stagnant' blood that has ceased to circulate properly. It underlies many gynecological disorders in Korean medicine theory. Ikmo's primary action is described as resolving 어혈, making it especially valuable for postpartum conditions where blood stagnation is considered the root pathology.

entities_mentioned.name: 쑥 / 艾 (Ssuk / Ài)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia princeps Pamp. or Artemisia argyi H.Lév. & Vaniot (Korean/Chinese mugwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited here only as a morphological reference point: the Dongui Bogam describes ikmo's leaves as resembling mugwort leaves, aiding identification in the field. Artemisia princeps is the dominant mugwort species on the Korean peninsula and is itself a major herb in the Dongui Bogam for warming the uterus and stopping bleeding.

results.translator_notes: 1. KOREAN VS. CHINESE DIVERGENCE: While ikmo/Yimu cao is well-attested in Chinese materia medica (notably Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu, 本草綱目, 1596 — compiled just before the Dongui Bogam), Heo Jun's treatment gives markedly greater clinical weight to its postpartum applications, reflecting a Korean medical culture in which women's postpartum recovery (산후조리, sanhujori) was a central healthcare concern. Chinese sources tend to emphasize its seed (茺蔚子, Chungwi-ja / Chōng Wèi Zǐ) equally or more; the Dongui Bogam privileges the whole herb, especially the aerial parts. 2. RITUAL HARVEST TIMING: The specification of the fifth day of the fifth lunar month (단오, Dano) as an optimal harvest time reflects Korean folk-medicinal cosmology in which plants gathered on auspicious calendrical dates carry heightened therapeutic power — a syncretic blend of Daoist, Buddhist, and indigenous Korean shamanistic beliefs that Heo Jun incorporated into his otherwise rationalist, systematized compendium. 3. MORPHOLOGICAL NOTE: The square stem noted in the source is a reliable field identification feature of the Lamiaceae (mint family), to which Leonurus belongs, and demonstrates the Dongui Bogam's practical attention to botanical identification accessible to practitioners without formal training. 4. ORIGINAL TEXT RECONSTRUCTION: The source segment provided was a prompt/summary rather than a verbatim classical Korean/Chinese passage. The 'original_text' field above has been reconstructed in period-appropriate literary Korean and Classical Chinese hybrid style consistent with the Dongui Bogam's actual textual register (湯液篇 / Tangyak-pyeon, Materia Medica section). Readers consulting primary sources should refer to the 동의보감 탕액편 (Dongui Bogam, Tangyak-pyeon), 草部 (Herb Division), ikmo entry.
