entities_mentioned.notes: Somewhat unusual inclusion in a Kidney Yang tonic, as Chajeonja is primarily diuretic and Yin-benefiting in standard TCM. In Korean formulation theory, its inclusion was understood to guide turbid pathogenic fluids downward and out, thereby clarifying the Kidney channel without injuring Essence — a nuanced Korean interpretive tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 토사자 (菟絲子) — Tosaja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cuscuta chinensis Lam. / Cuscuta japonica Choisy (dodder seed)
entities_mentioned.notes: A parasitic vine whose seeds are a premier Kidney tonic in both Korean and Chinese medicine; supplements both Yin and Yang without being excessively warming. Dongui Bogam notes Korean-harvested Cuscuta japonica as preferable for local preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: 오보자 (五補子) — Oboja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound formula (not a single botanical species)
entities_mentioned.notes: The formula name 'Five Supplementing Seeds' [五補子] is recorded in the Dongui Bogam internal medicine chapters dealing with Kidney deficiency and male reproductive decline. The 'five seeds' structure has mnemonic and cosmological significance, possibly alluding to the Five Phases [五行] correspondence with Kidney Water, though the formula is not strictly mapped to five-phase theory in Heo Jun's text.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a modern descriptive prompt rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam primary passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed in period-appropriate Sino-Korean medical register based on standard Dongui Bogam conventions and the formula's known contents. The Oboja formula illustrates a key feature of Korean medical practice as codified by Heo Jun: the deliberate substitution or specification of Korean-native plant species (most notably Rubus coreanus in place of Chinese Rubus chingii) and the broader project of the Dongui Bogam to document 'Eastern [Korean] medicine' [東醫] as distinct from Chinese models. The inclusion of Chajeonja (Plantago seed) in a Yang-supplementing formula is a point of interpretive interest: Korean commentators justified it as a 'guiding herb' [引經藥] that prevents the warming agents from generating pathogenic heat. Practitioners should note that Sasangja (Cnidium seed) carries cautions in cases of Yin deficiency with effulgent fire [陰虛火旺]. Dosage and preparation details follow standard Dongui Bogam pill-making conventions: ingredients dried, ground to powder, bound with refined honey, formed into pills the size of wutong seeds [梧桐子大], and taken with warm salted water [鹽湯] on an empty stomach to direct the medicinals to the Kidney channel — a specifically Korean pharmaceutical convention emphasized by Heo Jun.

results.title_inferred: Ogeopi (Five-Bark Spiny Shrub Bark) — Acanthopanax / Siberian Ginseng Bark
results.original_text: 오계피 (五加皮) — Acanthopanax senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Harms. 오가피(五加皮)라고도 함. 맛은 맵고 쓰며 성질은 따뜻하다[辛苦溫]. 독이 없다. 풍습(風濕)으로 인한 사지(四肢) 마비와 동통(疼痛), 근골(筋骨) 연약, 하지(下肢) 위약(痿弱), 소아(小兒)의 보행 지연, 음위(陰痿), 요슬(腰膝) 동통을 치료한다. 오래 복용하면 몸이 가벼워지고 늙지 않는다고 하였다.
results.translation: Ogeopi (五加皮, 'Five-Additions Bark') — also written Ogapi (오가피). Flavor: acrid and bitter; Nature: warm [辛苦溫]. Non-toxic. Indicated for: paralysis and pain of the four limbs caused by Wind-Dampness [風濕]; weakness and softness of the sinews and bones [筋骨]; weakness of the lower extremities [下肢痿弱]; delayed walking in children [小兒步行遲延]; impotence [陰痿]; and pain of the lumbar region and knees [腰膝疼痛]. Prolonged use is said to lighten the body and prevent aging [久服輕身不老].

entities_mentioned.name: 오계피 / 오가피 (五加皮, Ogeopi / Ogapi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acanthopanax senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Harms (Siberian ginseng / Eleuthero); also closely associated with Acanthopanax sessiliflorus and related Acanthopanax spp. native to Korea
entities_mentioned.notes: The Korean term 오계피 appears to be a variant romanization/transcription of 오가피 (五加皮), referring to the dried root bark or stem bark of Acanthopanax species. In the Dongui Bogam, Heo Jun distinguishes Korean-sourced Acanthopanax (particularly A. sessiliflorus and A. senticosus, abundant in Korean mountain forests) as superior in potency to Chinese material — a notable point of divergence from mainstream Ming-dynasty Chinese pharmacopeia, which more commonly referenced A. gracilistylus. Korean traditional medicine particularly prized the bark from wild high-altitude specimens. The five-leaflet form of the plant is considered the finest grade, hence the name 五加 ('five additions/enhancements').

entities_mentioned.name: 風濕 (Pungsŭp / Wind-Dampness)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pathogenic factor combination in East Asian medicine; clinically correlates with rheumatic/musculoskeletal inflammatory conditions
entities_mentioned.notes: A core diagnostic category in Korean and Chinese medicine theory; treatment with warming, dispersing herbs such as Ogeopi was standard.

entities_mentioned.name: 筋骨 (Singol / Sinews and Bones)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: A functional tissue category in Korean medicine theory; Ogeopi was considered a primary tonic for the Liver and Kidney organ systems, which govern sinews and bones respectively.

entities_mentioned.name: 陰痿 (Ŭmwi / Impotence)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erectile dysfunction / male sexual insufficiency
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated here as a Kidney Yang deficiency presentation, consistent with Ogeopi's warm nature and tonic action on the Kidney.

entities_mentioned.name: 久服輕身不老 (Prolonged use lightens the body and prevents aging)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Daoist-influenced longevity claim recurring throughout the Dongui Bogam for superior tonic herbs; reflects the influence of Shenong Bencao Jing and Daoist yangsheng (養生) cultivation traditions on Heo Jun's compendium.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment label uses the spelling 'Ogeopi' (오계피) which appears to be either a regional variant transcription or a minor OCR/scribal error for the standard 오가피 (五加皮, Ogapi). Both forms refer to the same herb and the Chinese character compound 五加皮 is unambiguous. This entry treats them as identical. 2. The source text as supplied is a modern scholarly summary rather than a verbatim Dongui Bogam manuscript passage; the original 1613 text would be in Classical Chinese (Hanja) with Korean idu/hangul glosses. The translation above renders the medical content faithfully according to Dongui Bogam conventions. 3. Acanthopanax senticosus was later branded 'Siberian ginseng' in 20th-century Western commerce; this name is not used in Heo Jun's period. The Dongui Bogam would not use this modern marketing term. 4. Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (especially the 'Tangaek (湯液)' chapters) emphasizes locally sourced Korean Acanthopanax as medicinally superior and more readily available than imported Chinese material, consistent with the Dongui Bogam's broader project of asserting Korean pharmaceutical self-sufficiency (鄕藥, hyangyak). 5. Key formula contexts in the Dongui Bogam involving Ogapi include: Ogapi-ju (五加皮酒, a medicinal wine for Wind-Dampness bi-syndrome and sinew weakness) and compound formulas for paediatric motor developmental delay — the latter being a notably prominent Korean clinical application.

results.title_inferred: Omae (烏梅) — Smoked Plum: Astringent, Antitussive, and Thirst-Relieving Herb
results.original_text: 오매 (烏梅) — Prunus mume — 맛은 시고 성질은 평하며 독이 없다. 폐를 수렴하고 기침을 그치게 하며 갈증을 멈추고 설사를 그치게 한다. 또한 회충을 죽이고 구역질을 멈추게 한다.
results.translation:
Omae (烏梅, 'Black/Smoked Plum') — Prunus mume

Flavor: sour. Nature: balanced [平, neutral temperature]. Toxicity: none.

Therapeutic Actions (according to Korean medicine theory):
• Astringes [收斂] the Lung; arrests coughing.
• Stops thirst; generates fluids [生津].
• Arrests diarrhea and dysentery by astringing the intestines [澁腸固脫].
• Kills roundworms [蛔蟲] and quiets the visceral agitation they cause.
• Stops vomiting and retching.
• Relieves summerheat thirst when decocted as a beverage.

Preparation Methods:
1. Smoked / carbonised plum (standard omae): Unripe green plums (Prunus mume) are smoked over straw or wheat-chaff fire until the flesh turns black. This is the classical 烏梅 preparation used in decoctions.
2. Omae-tang (烏梅湯): Omae simmered with sugar or honey as a cooling summer beverage to relieve thirst and heat.
3. Omae-hwan (烏梅丸 / Wumei Wan): Omae combined with Xixin (細辛), Ganjiang (乾薑), Hwangnyeon (黃連), Hwangbaek (黃柏), Gyepi (桂皮), Buja (附子), Cheoncho (川椒), Danggwi (當歸), and Ginseng (人蔘) — the classical formula for jiǎo (蛔厥), i.e., roundworm-induced cold limbs and vomiting. This formula appears in both the Shanghanlun and the Dongui Bogam and was widely employed in Joseon clinical practice.
4. Topical paste: Charred omae (烏梅炭) ground with vinegar applied to corns, warts, and necrotic flesh to promote dissolution of hardened tissue.

Key Formulas in Dongui Bogam:
• 烏梅丸 (Omae-hwan): For hui-jue (蛔厥) — roundworm colic with cold extremities.
• 固腸丸 (Gojang-hwan): Omae with Aconite and Dried Ginger for chronic cold-pattern diarrhea.
• 生津止渴湯: Omae combined with Maekmundong (麥門冬) and Chwigeun (取根) herbs for febrile thirst and Lung-dryness cough.

Korean-Specific and Joseon Medical Tradition Notes:
• Omae was cultivated in the southern regions of the Korean peninsula (Jeolla and Gyeongsang provinces), and Heo Jun acknowledges domestically sourced plums as therapeutically valid, representing a nativist [鄕藥, hyangyak] orientation that distinguishes the Dongui Bogam from purely China-referencing texts.
• The Dongui Bogam categorises omae under the Fruit Medicinals [果部] section and emphasises its role in summer medicine — a reflection of Korea's humid continental summers and the prevalence of summerheat [暑] disorders in Joseon society.
• Korean practice tended to use omae-tang as a popular household drink for heatstroke prevention, a usage more culturally embedded in Korea than in contemporaneous Chinese medical texts.
• Divergence from Chinese TCM: While Chinese texts (e.g., Bencao Gangmu, 1596 CE) focus heavily on omae's antiparasitic and astringent applications, Heo Jun places comparatively greater emphasis on its fluid-generating [生津] and Lung-astringing [斂肺] roles, aligning with the Dongui Bogam's broader thematic concern with preserving the body's essential fluids [精] — a distinctly Korean theoretical emphasis.

entities_mentioned.name: 오매 / 烏梅 (Omae)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus mume (Siebold & Zucc.) — smoked or smoke-dried unripe fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: The smoked preparation is critical: fresh or ripe plum has different properties. 烏梅 specifically denotes the carbonised/smoked unripe fruit, which concentrates the sour-astringent principle (likely malic and citric acids, as well as tannins).

entities_mentioned.name: 烏梅丸 (Omae-hwan / Wumei Wan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Originates in Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghanlun (c. 200 CE); retained verbatim in Dongui Bogam. Used for roundworm colic (ascariasis-induced biliary or intestinal spasm). Omae's sourness is theorised to 'restrain' the worm, which is said to be calmed by sour and killed by bitter-pungent herbs in the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 細辛 (Xixin / Sesin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum sieboldii Miq. or Asarum heterotropoides — wild ginger root
entities_mentioned.notes: Component of Omae-hwan; pungent warming herb that disperses cold.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃連 (Hwangnyeon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis japonica or Coptis chinensis — goldthread rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Bitter-cold herb in Omae-hwan; theorised to kill or repel roundworms.

entities_mentioned.name: 附子 (Buja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debx. — processed aconite lateral root
entities_mentioned.notes: Warming yang herb used in Omae-hwan and cold-pattern diarrhea formulas. Requires careful processing to reduce aconitine toxicity.

entities_mentioned.name: 麥門冬 (Maekmundong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ophiopogon japonicus (L.f.) Ker Gawl.
entities_mentioned.notes: Yin-nourishing, fluid-generating herb paired with omae in thirst and Lung-dryness formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: 川椒 (Cheoncho)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zanthoxylum bungeanum Maxim. — Sichuan pepper
entities_mentioned.notes: Pungent-warm; antiparasitic component of Omae-hwan.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a compressed scholarly label rather than a full classical Korean or classical Chinese passage; accordingly, this entry has been reconstructed from the established content of the Dongui Bogam's 果部 (Fruit Medicinals) section on 烏梅, cross-referenced with the Hyangyak Jipseongbang and Bencao Gangmu for comparative context. All therapeutic claims and formula contents reflect period-accurate Joseon medical theory (four natures, five flavors, organ-channel tropism) and should not be read as modern medical advice. The Omae-hwan formula cited is classical and its roundworm-treatment rationale is based on humoral/qi theory; modern pharmacological research on Prunus mume does confirm antimicrobial and antispasmodic constituents (benzaldehyde, citric acid, malic acid, polyphenols), lending partial scientific correlation. The 'smoked plum' preparation method is essential to the identity of 烏梅 and distinguishes it from fresh 梅 (maesil) used in Korean culinary contexts such as maesil-cheong (plum syrup).

results.title_inferred: Oryeong-san (五苓散) — Five-Ingredient Poria Powder for Dampness Disorders
results.original_text: 오령산 (五苓散) — 五苓散 — 다섯 가지 약재로 이루어진 산제(散劑). 습(濕)을 다스리는 대표 처방. 택사(澤瀉), 저령(猪苓), 복령(茯苓), 백출(白朮), 계지(桂枝)로 구성됨.
results.translation:
Oryeong-san [五苓散, 'Five-Ling Powder'] is a classical powdered formula composed of five ingredients, widely recognized in Korean medicine [한의학] and its Chinese antecedent traditions as the principal prescription for resolving dampness [濕, seup] and promoting urination [利水]. The formula treats conditions of water accumulation [水滯], urinary difficulty [小便不利], edema [浮腫], thirst with inability to retain fluids, diarrhea associated with internal water retention, and headache or dizziness caused by retained fluids in the upper burner [上焦]. According to Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 1613 CE), this formula acts upon the Bladder [膀胱] and Spleen [脾] organ systems to warm yang [溫陽], transform dampness [化濕], and open the waterways [通利水道].

Composition:
1. 택사 (澤瀉, Taeksa) — Alisma orientale rhizome [水瀉根]: the chief herb [君藥]; strongly promotes urination and drains dampness downward.
2. 저령 (猪苓, Jeoryeong) — Polyporus umbellatus sclerotium: supports urination; drains water without injuring yin.
3. 복령 (茯苓, Bongnyeong) — Poria cocos sclerotium [白茯苓]: harmonizes the middle burner [中焦], calms the spirit, and transforms dampness.
4. 백출 (白朮, Baekchul) — Atractylodes macrocephala rhizome: strengthens the Spleen [健脾] to resolve dampness at its root.
5. 계지 (桂枝, Gyeji) — Cinnamomum cassia twig [肉桂枝]: warms yang, unblocks the bladder qi transformation [膀胱氣化], and disperses cold-damp.

Preparation: Grind all five ingredients into a fine powder [散]. Administer with warm water or rice-water gruel [米飮]. In some Korean adaptations recorded in the Dongui Bogam, the formula is also decocted as a concentrated tea [湯], referred to colloquially as 오령탕 [五苓湯], particularly for pediatric use or when the patient is unable to swallow powder.

Indications per Dongui Bogam:
— Primary: retention of water-dampness [水濕停滯] in the lower burner with difficult urination [小便不利]
— Thirst [煩渴] with nausea after drinking fluids (water counterflow [水逆])
— Generalized edema [全身浮腫] and abdominal distension [腹脹]
— Summer-heat [暑濕] disorder with vomiting, diarrhea, and thirst
— Headache and vertigo from phlegm-fluid [痰飮] retention

Korean-Specific Observations (divergence from Chinese TCM):
Heo Jun emphasized the use of Oryeong-san in combination with indigenous Korean flora. Notably, he recommended pairing the formula with 율무 [薏苡仁, Job's tears — Coix lacryma-jobi], which is cultivated widely on the Korean peninsula, to enhance the leaching of dampness [滲濕] particularly in cases of bi-syndrome [痺證] with swollen joints aggravated by damp climates. Heo Jun also notes the appropriateness of this formula for Korea's humid mountain-valley geography [山谷濕地], where damp-cold disorders differ in character from the damp-heat patterns more commonly treated in southern Chinese medicine. Korean practice favored a slightly higher proportion of 백출 relative to 택사 compared to the Song dynasty proportions, reflecting the colder Korean climate and the consequent need to more strongly fortify Spleen yang.

entities_mentioned.name: 택사 / 澤瀉 (Taeksa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma orientale (Sam.) Juz. — Oriental water plantain rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Chief herb [君藥] of the formula; strongly diuretic; drains dampness downward via the Bladder channel. Widely cultivated in Korea and China.

entities_mentioned.name: 저령 / 猪苓 (Jeoryeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyporus umbellatus (Pers.) Fr. — sclerotium of polypore fungus
entities_mentioned.notes: Fungal sclerotium used as a deputy herb [臣藥]; promotes urination without damaging yin fluids. Found in Korean mountain forests.

entities_mentioned.name: 복령 / 茯苓 (Bongnyeong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia cocos (F.A.Wolf) Ryvarden & Gilb. (syn. Poria cocos) — sclerotium of Poria fungus
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important herbs in Korean and Chinese medicine. Calms the spirit [安神], strengthens the Spleen, and transforms dampness. Grown in Korean highland pine forests.

entities_mentioned.name: 백출 / 白朮 (Baekchul)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. — large-headed atractylodes rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Strengthens Spleen qi [健脾益氣] and dries dampness [燥濕]. Korean texts including the Dongui Bogam distinguish 백출 (white atractylodes) from 창출 (grey/black atractylodes, Atractylodes lancea), with 백출 preferred for tonification.

entities_mentioned.name: 계지 / 桂枝 (Gyeji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia (L.) J.Presl — cinnamon twig
entities_mentioned.notes: Warms yang and activates bladder qi transformation [膀胱氣化]. Some Korean formularies substitute 육계 [肉桂, cinnamon bark] for stronger warming when cold-damp predominates, a noted divergence from classical Chinese usage of the twig alone.

entities_mentioned.name: 율무 / 薏苡仁 (Yulmu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi L. var. ma-yuen (Rom.Caill.) Stapf — Job's tears seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Not part of the classical five-ingredient formula, but added in Korean adaptations per Heo Jun for enhanced dampness-leaching [滲濕] and joint-swelling relief. Cultivated extensively on the Korean peninsula; represents a uniquely Korean pharmaceutical addition to this formula's clinical application.

entities_mentioned.name: 오령산 / 五苓散 (Oryeong-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound herbal formula — no single plant equivalent
entities_mentioned.notes: Originally from the Shanghan Lun [傷寒論] by Zhang Zhongjing (c. 200 CE). Adopted comprehensively into Korean medicine and discussed in the Dongui Bogam under the category of dampness [濕] disorders. Remains one of the most commonly prescribed classical formulas in contemporary Korean medicine [한의학].

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment provides only a brief label and thematic tag ('damp'); the full entry has been reconstructed from canonical Dongui Bogam content regarding Oryeong-san as it appears in the Japbyeong-pyeon [雜病篇] sections on dampness [濕], urinary disorders [小便], and water/fluid retention [水濕]. Scholars should cross-reference the Dongui Bogam Book 5 (內景篇 — Inner Landscape) and Book 11–12 (雜病篇 — Miscellaneous Diseases) for primary source passages.
2. The formula originates in Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghan Lun and Jingui Yaolüe; Heo Jun's contribution is his contextualization of the formula within Korean geographic and climatic conditions, as well as his integration of indigenous Korean plant knowledge.
3. 'Five Ling' [五苓] refers to the five ingredients that contain or relate to 'ling' [苓] — a character associated with spirit-fungus medicines (茯苓, 猪苓) — though the full formula includes non-'ling' herbs; this naming is conventional rather than strictly botanical.
4. Modern pharmacological studies confirm diuretic and renal-protective effects of the formula's components, particularly Alisma and Poria, supporting traditional indications.
5. Korean-specific divergences (higher 백출 ratio, substitution of 육계 for 계지, addition of 율무) are documented in Korean medical compendia but may not appear verbatim in all editions of the Dongui Bogam; some are inferred from Heo Jun's broader clinical philosophy as expressed across the text.

results.title_inferred: Pyeong Wi San (平胃散) — Stomach-Regulating Powder: Digestive Formula
results.original_text: 平胃散 평위산 — 위(胃)를 고르게 하는 산제(散劑). 소화를 돕고 습(濕)을 제거하며 비위(脾胃)를 조화롭게 함.
results.translation:
Pyeong Wi San (平胃散, 'Stomach-Leveling Powder') is a classical compound formula [散劑, powdered medicinal preparation] recorded in Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 1613 CE). Its primary therapeutic actions are: to harmonize and regulate the Spleen and Stomach [脾胃, bi-wi], to dry dampness [燥濕, jo-seup], to move Qi and relieve stagnation [行氣, haeng-gi], and to aid digestion [消食, so-sik].

CORE COMPOSITION (standard formulation as cited in Dongui Bogam, derived from the Song-dynasty Hejiju Fang 和劑局方):
•창출 (蒼朮, Cangzhu) — Atractylodes lancea rhizome: 4 don [錢]; the principal herb; dries dampness, strengthens the Spleen.
• 후박 (厚朴, Houpo) — Magnolia officinalis bark: 3 don; moves Qi, disperses fullness, dries dampness.
• 진피 (陳皮, Chenpi) — Citrus reticulata aged peel: 3 don; regulates Qi, transforms phlegm-damp.
• 감초 (甘草, Gancao) — Glycyrrhiza uralensis root, honey-fried [炙甘草]: 1 don; harmonizes the formula, tonifies the Middle Burner [中焦].
• 생강 (生薑, Shengjiang) — Zingiber officinale fresh rhizome: 3 slices; warming adjuvant.
• 대조 (大棗, Dazao) — Ziziphus jujuba fruit: 2 pieces; tonifies the Middle, harmonizes.

PREPARATION: All ingredients (except ginger and jujube) are ground into a coarse powder [粗末]. Decocted in water with fresh ginger and jujube until reduced. Taken warm before meals, or prepared as a fine powder and taken with warm water or ginger decoction.

INDICATIONS (conditions treated):
1. Spleen-Stomach Damp Stagnation [脾胃濕滯]: epigastric and abdominal fullness and distension.
2. Poor appetite, nausea, belching, acid reflux.
3. Loose stools or irregular bowel movements due to Spleen Qi deficiency with damp accumulation.
4. Heavy, fatigued limbs and general lassitude [濕困四肢].
5. A thick, greasy tongue coating [苔厚膩] with a slippery pulse [脈滑] — hallmark presentation.

KOREAN-SPECIFIC ADAPTATIONS (divergence from Chinese TCM practice):
• Heo Jun emphasizes the use of Korean-cultivated 창출 (Atractylodes lancea, 蒼朮), which was considered by Korean physicians to be more potent in drying dampness than the Chinese variety. Korean 창출 from mountain regions (especially the Gangwon and Chungcheong provinces) was treated as a superior-grade substitute.
• The Dongui Bogam records modified versions: 'Gami Pyeong Wi San' (加味平胃散), with additions such as 신국 (神麴, Massa Fermentata) and 맥아 (麥芽, Hordeum vulgare malt) for stronger digestive action — reflecting Korean dietary patterns heavy in grains and fermented foods.
• Korean practice frequently combined Pyeong Wi San with 이진탕 (二陳湯, Yijin Tang) to form 평진탕 (平陳湯), targeting phlegm-damp with simultaneous Stomach regulation — a combination less emphasized in mainstream Chinese formularies of the same period.
• Heo Jun notes that in Korean climatic conditions (cold, damp winters; humid summers), Spleen-Stomach damp stagnation was endemic, making Pyeong Wi San one of the most broadly applied base formulas [基本方] in Korean internal medicine [內景篇, Naegyeong chapter].

SOURCE SECTION: Dongui Bogam, 內景篇 (Naegyeong Pyeon — Inner Landscape Chapter), 脾臟門 (Bi-jang mun — Spleen Viscera Section).

entities_mentioned.name: 창출 (蒼朮, Cangzhu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes lancea (Thunb.) DC. — rhizome (Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal herb of the formula. Korean physicians prized domestically cultivated 창출 over Chinese imports, considering the Korean variety more aromatic and more effective at drying dampness [燥濕]. Distinguished from 백출 (白朮, Atractylodes macrocephala) which tonifies rather than dries.

entities_mentioned.name: 후박 (厚朴, Houpo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia officinalis Rehder & E.H.Wilson — bark (Magnoliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Moves Qi downward, disperses abdominal fullness [消脹滿]. In Korean practice, also used as a standalone remedy for Qi stagnation in the chest and abdomen.

entities_mentioned.name: 진피 (陳皮, Chenpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco — aged dried peel (Rutaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Must be aged (陳, 'chen' = old) for at least one year to reduce drying properties and enhance Qi-moving efficacy. Korean texts specify 3-year aged peel as optimal.

entities_mentioned.name: 감초 (甘草, Gancao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. ex DC. — root (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in honey-fried form [炙甘草] to tonify the Middle Burner and harmonize the formula, preventing the drying herbs from injuring Stomach Yin.

entities_mentioned.name: 생강 (生薑, Shengjiang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe — fresh rhizome (Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard warming adjuvant in Korean and Chinese formularies. Enhances the Spleen-warming effect and counteracts nausea.

entities_mentioned.name: 대조 (大棗, Dazao)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. — dried fruit (Rhamnaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tonifies Middle Qi, harmonizes formula components. Ubiquitous in Korean traditional medicine as a harmonizing and nourishing adjuvant.

entities_mentioned.name: 신국 (神麴, Massa Fermentata)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Massa Medicata Fermentata — a fermented preparation of wheat flour with various herbs (Poaceae base)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added in the Korean-modified variant 'Gami Pyeong Wi San.' Strongly aids digestion of starchy foods [消穀食]. Reflects Korean dietary reliance on fermented grain products such as doenjang and ganjang.

entities_mentioned.name: 맥아 (麥芽, Mainya)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L. — germinated barley (Poaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added in augmented formula for grain-food stagnation. Also known to gently descend Liver Qi; Korean physicians used it broadly for digestive complaints in farmers consuming heavy grain diets.

entities_mentioned.name: 평진탕 (平陳湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound formula: Pyeong Wi San + Yijin Tang — no single botanical
entities_mentioned.notes: A distinctly Korean formulary combination merging Pyeong Wi San with 이진탕 (二陳湯). Targets concurrent Spleen-Stomach damp stagnation and phlegm-damp accumulation. Less documented in mainstream Chinese Song/Ming-dynasty formularies, suggesting Korean clinical innovation.

entities_mentioned.name: Pyeong Wi San (平胃散)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula — no single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Originally from Chinese Song-dynasty Hejiju Fang (和劑局方, Imperial Grace Formulary, 1107 CE). Adopted and extensively modified by Korean physicians. Recorded in Dongui Bogam as one of the foundational formulas for Spleen-Stomach disorders. Remains in active clinical use in Korean traditional medicine (韓醫學) today.

results.translator_notes: Pyeong Wi San is not a single herb but a canonical multi-herb formula [方劑, bangjei]. It is included here as a Plantacopia entry because the Dongui Bogam frequently presents formulas alongside individual medicinal substances within organ-system chapters (內景篇), and the formula's constituent plants carry individual botanical significance. The source text provided was a generation prompt rather than a direct transcription of classical Korean or Literary Chinese; this entry has been reconstructed from the Dongui Bogam's Spleen chapter (脾臟門) and cross-referenced with the Song-dynasty Hejiju Fang original. Identification confidence for all constituent herbs is high, as they are unambiguously named and their classical descriptions match established pharmacognostic records. The Korean divergences noted — especially the premium placed on domestically sourced 창출 and the compound formula 평진탕 — reflect well-documented features of Joseon-period Korean medicine that distinguished it from contemporaneous Chinese practice. No genuinely illegible passages were encountered, as the source was a prompt-generated description rather than a scanned manuscript.
results._label: Dongui Bogam — Pyeong Wi San 평위산

results.title_inferred: Sagunja-tang (四君子湯) — Four Gentlemen Decoction: Foundational Qi-Tonifying Formula
results.original_text: 사군자탕 (四君子湯) — 四君子湯. 治面色萎白, 言語輕微, 四肢無力, 脈來虛弱者. 人蔘·白朮·白茯苓·甘草(炙) 各一錢. 右剉作一貼, 薑三片·棗二枚, 水煎服.
results.translation:
Sagunja-tang (四君子湯) — Four Gentlemen Decoction.

Indications: Treats pallid or sallow complexion, faint and weak speech, lack of strength in the four limbs, and a vacuous, weak pulse.

Composition: Insamn [人蔘, Ginseng root], Baekchul [白朮, White Atractylodes rhizome], Baekbongnyeong [白茯苓, White Poria fungus], Gamcho (炙) [甘草, Honey-fried Licorice root] — each 1 pun [錢, approximately 3.75 g].

Preparation and administration: Coarsely slice and prepare as a single packet [一貼]. Add 3 slices of fresh ginger [生薑] and 2 jujube fruits [大棗]. Decoct in water and take orally.

Formula rationale (as understood within Dongui Bogam tradition): This formula addresses deficiency [虛] of Spleen-Stomach qi [脾胃之氣]. Ginseng [人蔘] serves as the chief drug [君藥], powerfully supplementing Original Qi [元氣]; White Atractylodes [白朮] strengthens the Spleen and dries Dampness [健脾燥濕]; Poria [茯苓] calms the mind and leaches out Dampness [寧心滲濕]; Honey-fried Licorice [炙甘草] harmonizes all the drugs and supplements the Middle Burner [補中和藥]. The four herbs together constitute a balanced, moderate ('gentlemanly') combination that supplements Spleen-Stomach qi without harshness. Ginger and Jujube are added as envoy herbs to regulate the Nutritive and Protective Qi [調和營衛].

entities_mentioned.name: 人蔘 (Insam / Rénshēn) — Ginseng
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey. (family Araliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The chief herb (君藥) of this formula. Korean-grown Ginseng (朝鮮人蔘) was considered by Heo Jun to be among the finest quality in the world and was a point of significant pride in the Dongui Bogam, which strongly advocated for the use of domestically sourced Korean ginseng (鄕藥材) over imported Chinese varieties. This is a key divergence from standard Chinese TCM sourcing practice of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: 白朮 (Baekchul / Báizhú) — White Atractylodes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (family Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Deputy herb (臣藥). Strengthens the Spleen [健脾] and dries Dampness [燥濕]. The Dongui Bogam at times distinguishes between Baekchul and Changchul (蒼朮, Grey/Black Atractylodes, A. lancea), using Baekchul preferentially for supplementing Spleen qi.

entities_mentioned.name: 白茯苓 (Baekbongnyeong / Báifúlíng) — White Poria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns, syn. Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf (family Polyporaceae) — sclerotium
entities_mentioned.notes: Assistant herb (佐藥). Fortifies the Spleen, percolates Dampness [滲濕], and quiets the Heart-spirit [寧心]. The 'white' inner portion of the Poria sclerotium is specified, distinguishing it from Poria with pine root (茯神, bongsin) used more specifically for calming the spirit.

entities_mentioned.name: 炙甘草 (Jaek Gamcho / Zhì Gāncǎo) — Honey-fried Licorice
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. (family Fabaceae), root processed with honey
entities_mentioned.notes: Envoy/harmonizing herb (使藥). Honey-frying [炙] enhances its Spleen-supplementing and qi-tonifying properties compared to raw licorice. Acts to harmonize the actions of all other herbs in the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: 生薑 (Saenggang / Shēngjiāng) — Fresh Ginger
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (family Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard adjuvant added during decoction in many Korean and Chinese classical formulas. Warms the Stomach, aids transportation, and prevents the cloying nature of tonic herbs from causing stagnation.

entities_mentioned.name: 大棗 (Daejo / Dàzǎo) — Jujube
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ziziphus jujuba Mill. (family Rhamnaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with fresh ginger as standard envoy combination. Nourishes the Blood, moderates the formula, and protects the Spleen-Stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: 사군자탕 (Sagunja-tang / 四君子湯) — Four Gentlemen Decoction
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A classical compound formula, not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most foundational formulas in East Asian medicine. Classified under Qi-supplementing formulas (補氣劑). Considered the base formula from which numerous other important formulas are derived, including Yukunja-tang (六君子湯, Six Gentlemen Decoction, adding Banha and Chinpi), Samnyeong Baekchul-san (蔘苓白朮散), and Bojungikgi-tang (補中益氣湯). Its name alludes to the 'four gentlemen' (四君子) of classical Chinese culture — plum, orchid, chrysanthemum, and bamboo — implying a balanced, refined, and moderate character.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is a brief formula stub rather than a full classical passage with extended commentary; the translation has been expanded using established Dongui Bogam formula entries and Korean medical tradition to produce a complete Plantacopia-standard entry.

2. KOREAN DIVERGENCE FROM CHINESE TCM: The Dongui Bogam (1613) places exceptional emphasis on Korean-sourced ginseng (朝鮮人蔘 / 鄕藥 hyangyak, 'local medicines') as superior to or equivalent to Chinese-sourced material. Heo Jun's broader project was explicitly to establish Korean medical sovereignty by demonstrating that Korean domestic flora could supply all necessary materia medica, reducing dependence on expensive imported Chinese drugs. This patriotic-medical stance is a defining feature of the Dongui Bogam and marks a significant methodological and ideological departure from contemporary Chinese medical texts.

3. DOSAGE NOTE: '各一錢' (each 1 pun/錢) = approximately 3.75 g per herb in the Ming-Joseon measurement system, yielding approximately 15 g total crude drug per packet before ginger and jujube.

4. The formula originates from the Chinese Song dynasty text Heji Jufang (和劑局方, c. 1110 CE) but was thoroughly integrated into Korean medical practice by Heo Jun's era and is here presented within a Korean clinical and botanical context.

5. No OCR errors or illegible passages were detected in the source; the source text was a brief modern gloss rather than original Classical Chinese, so the original_text field has been reconstructed in canonical Classical Chinese per established textual sources.

results.title_inferred: Samuljang (四物湯): Four Substances Formula for Blood Disorders
results.original_text: 사물장 (四物湯) — 혈(血)을 보(補)하는 처방. 당귀(當歸), 천궁(川芎), 백작약(白芍藥), 숙지황(熟地黃) 각 등분(各等分). 혈허(血虛), 혈조(血燥), 월경불순(月經不順), 붕루(崩漏), 혈체(血滯) 등을 치료함.
results.translation: Samuljang [四物湯, 'Four Substances Decoction']: A foundational formula for supplementing and regulating blood [血, hyeol]. The four constituent herbs are: Danggwi [當歸, Angelica gigas or A. sinensis], Cheongung [川芎, Ligusticum chuanxiong], Baekjaknyak [白芍藥, Paeonia lactiflora, white peony root], and Sukjihwang [熟地黃, Rehmannia glutinosa, prepared/steamed root]. Each herb is taken in equal proportion [各等分]. This formula governs the treatment of blood vacuity [血虛, hyeolheo], blood dryness [血燥, hyeoljo], irregular menstruation [月經不順], uterine flooding and spotting [崩漏, bungnu], and blood stagnation [血滯, hyeolche]. It is considered the root formula [祖方] for all blood-phase disorders in Korean and East Asian medicine, representing one of the two foundational pillars of internal medicine alongside the Four Gentlemen Decoction [四君子湯, Sagunjatang] for qi vacuity. In the Dongui Bogam, Heo Jun classifies this formula under the 'Blood' [血] section of the Internal Landscape [內景篇, Naegyeong-pyeon], underscoring its role in nourishing, moving, and cooling the blood in a balanced manner.

entities_mentioned.name: Danggwi (당귀, 當歸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean angelica) — note: Korean practice preferentially uses A. gigas, while Chinese TCM uses A. sinensis (Chinese angelica). This is a meaningful divergence.
entities_mentioned.notes: In the Dongui Bogam, Heo Jun explicitly favors the native Korean species Angelica gigas (참당귀), which has a distinctly different phytochemical profile (rich in decursin and decursinol angelate) compared to the Chinese A. sinensis. This is one of the clearest documented points where Korean medical practice diverges from Chinese TCM. Danggwi is classified as sweet, acrid, and warm [甘辛溫]; it nourishes blood [補血], invigorates blood [活血], and moderates pain [止痛]. It is considered the chief herb [君藥] of the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: Cheongung (천궁, 川芎)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort. (Sichuan lovage rhizome)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified as acrid and warm [辛溫]; invigorates blood and moves qi [活血行氣], dispels wind and alleviates pain [祛風止痛]. Acts as the envoy herb to move the blood supplemented by Danggwi, preventing stagnation. Caution is noted in cases of excessive heat or yin vacuity.

entities_mentioned.name: Baekjaknyak (백작약, 白芍藥)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (Chinese peony, white peony root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified as bitter, sour, and slightly cold [苦酸微寒]; nourishes blood and astringes yin [養血斂陰], moderates urgency and alleviates pain [緩急止痛], calms liver yang [平肝]. Provides the cooling, yin-nourishing counterbalance to the warmth of Danggwi and Cheongung in the formula. The Dongui Bogam distinguishes white peony [白芍] for nourishing and red peony [赤芍] for dispersing, a distinction consistent with both Korean and Chinese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Sukjihwang (숙지황, 熟地黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) DC., prepared/steamed root (as opposed to raw Saengji-hwang 生地黃)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified as sweet and slightly warm [甘微溫]; richly nourishes yin and supplements blood [滋陰補血], supplements essence and fills marrow [益精填髓]. The prepared form [熟地黃], steamed with rice wine, is warmer and more tonifying than the raw root [生地黃], which is cold and more appropriate for clearing heat from the blood. The Dongui Bogam specifies the prepared form for this blood-supplementing formula. Heo Jun notes that Korean-produced Rehmannia (Korean 지황, native to the peninsula) was cultivated and considered medicinally valid alongside imported Chinese material.

entities_mentioned.name: Samuljang / Samul-tang (사물장 / 四物湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Four Substances Decoction — a classical compound formula, not a single herb
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most fundamental formulas in the Korean and Chinese medical canon. In the Dongui Bogam, it serves as the base formula [基本方] from which numerous derivative formulas are generated: e.g., Palmultang [八物湯, Eight Substances Decoction] when combined with Sagunjatang [四君子湯]; Sipjeondaebotang [十全大補湯, All-Inclusive Great Tonification Decoction] when further augmented; and Ongyeong-tang [溫經湯] and others for gynecological disorders. Heo Jun organized the Dongui Bogam's discussion of blood under five functions: generation [生血], supplementation [補血], cooling [凉血], stopping [止血], and moving [行血], with Samultang anchoring the supplementation and moving categories.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a prompt-generated synthesis rather than a direct verbatim OCR of a single Dongui Bogam page; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above reconstructs a plausible classical Korean/hanja formulation consistent with Heo Jun's style and the formula's documented content in the Dongui Bogam (Naegyeong-pyeon, Blood chapter [血門]). 
2. The most significant Korean-vs-Chinese divergence here is the substitution of Angelica gigas (Korean danggwi, 참당귀) for Angelica sinensis (Chinese danggwi, 중국당귀). Heo Jun's project in the Dongui Bogam was explicitly nationalistic in part — he sought to identify Korean-grown substitutes for Chinese herbs, and danggwi is a prime example. Modern phytochemical research confirms that A. gigas contains decursin and decursinol angelate as dominant coumarins, whereas A. sinensis is richer in Z-ligustilide and ferulic acid, representing genuinely distinct pharmacological profiles. 
3. The label 'Samuljang' in the source may reflect a dialectal or archaic Korean pronunciation/transcription variant; the standard rendering is 'Samultang' (사물탕, 四物湯). The suffix -jang (장) may indicate a paste or preserved preparation variant rather than a standard decoction [湯], though without additional context this cannot be confirmed definitively. 
4. Equal proportions [各等分] is the classical prescription; clinical modifications routinely adjust ratios based on predominance of vacuity, stagnation, heat, or cold in the patient's blood pattern.

results.title_inferred: Samul-tang (Four Materials Decoction) — Blood Tonic Formula
results.original_text: 사물탕 (四物湯) — 혈을 보하는 기본 처방. 당귀 (當歸), 천궁 (川芎), 백작약 (白芍藥), 숙지황 (熟地黃) 각 일전. 혈허 (血虛), 혈병 (血病), 월경불순 (月經不順), 붕루 (崩漏) 등을 치료한다. 위 약재를 물에 달여 복용한다.
results.translation:
Samul-tang (四物湯, 'Four Materials Decoction') is the foundational formula [kibang, 基方] for supplementing and nourishing blood [bo-hyeol, 補血] in Korean medicine. It consists of four equal-part ingredients — Danggwi [當歸, Angelica gigas], Cheongung [川芎, Ligusticum chuanxiong], Baek-jakyak [白芍藥, Paeonia lactiflora root, white peony], and Sukjihwang [熟地黃, Rehmannia glutinosa, prepared/steamed root] — each typically one jeon [一錢, approx. 3.75 g] in weight.

Indications: Blood vacuity [hyeol-heo, 血虛], general blood disorders [hyeol-byeong, 血病], irregular menstruation [wolgyeong-bulsun, 月經不順], and uterine bleeding or flooding and spotting [bungnu, 崩漏]. The formula addresses pallor, dizziness, palpitations, dry skin, and menstrual irregularities arising from insufficient or stagnant blood.

Preparation: The four herbs are decocted together in water and taken orally as a warm drink. Standard practice in the Dongui Bogam context recommends taking the strained decoction in divided doses.

Therapeutic logic (Korean medicine theory): Samul-tang operates on the principle of simultaneously nourishing [boyang, 補養] and moving [haenghyeol, 行血] the blood. Sukjihwang enriches and supplements the blood-essence [jeong-hyeol, 精血]; Danggwi nourishes and invigorates blood circulation; Baek-jakyak softens the liver and restrains yin; Cheongung moves qi within the blood, preventing stagnation from the enriching properties of the other three herbs. Together they achieve 'nourish without stagnating, move without dispersing.'

Key derived formulas in the Dongui Bogam:
1. Ssanghwa-tang (雙和湯): Samul-tang plus Hwanggi (黃芪, Astragalus) and Ganccho (甘草, licorice) — used post-illness or post-partum to tonify both qi and blood.
2. Taeum-tang (胎飮湯) / various gestational formulas: Samul-tang base modified for obstetric use.
3. Palljin-tang (八珍湯): Samul-tang combined with Sagunja-tang (四君子湯) — tonifies both qi and blood simultaneously.
4. Yukmijihwang-tang variants: Sometimes combined with Samul-tang base in gynaecological conditions.

Korean divergence from Chinese TCM: Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (1613) substitutes the locally cultivated Korean Danggwi (Angelica gigas Nakai) for the Chinese Danggwi (Angelica sinensis). Korean Angelica gigas has a distinctly higher concentration of decursin and decursinol angelate, alkaloids uncommon in the Chinese species, giving it somewhat different pharmacological properties. Korean practice also frequently uses a higher proportion of Sukjihwang relative to other ingredients in cold-constitution patients [soeum-in, 少陰人], reflecting Joseon-period individualized constitution medicine (Sasang typology, though fully codified later by Lee Je-ma). Heo Jun further contextualizes Samul-tang within the broader heuristic of 'blood being the mother of qi' [hyeol wi qi-ji-mo, 血爲氣之母], emphasizing its use in women's medicine [fujin-gwa, 婦人科] more systematically than contemporary Chinese texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Danggwi (당귀, 當歸)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica gigas Nakai (Korean variety); cf. Angelica sinensis (Chinese variety)
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean Angelica gigas is the standard substitute in Joseon-era practice. Contains decursin and decursinol angelate, pharmacologically distinct from the Chinese species. Used as the primary blood-nourishing and blood-invigorating herb in the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: Cheongung (천궁, 川芎)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ligusticum chuanxiong Hort. (syn. Cnidium officinale Makino in Korean use)
entities_mentioned.notes: Korean practice often uses Cnidium officinale Makino as a local analogue to Chinese Ligusticum chuanxiong. Functions to move blood and qi [haeng-gi-haeng-hyeol, 行氣行血], preventing stagnation within the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: Baek-jakyak (백작약, 白芍藥)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (white peony root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes blood, softens the liver, and alleviates pain. The 'white' (baek) variety is specified as opposed to red peony (jeok-jakyak, 赤芍藥) to emphasize supplementing rather than clearing blood.

entities_mentioned.name: Sukjihwang (숙지황, 熟地黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa Libosch., prepared root (steamed and dried repeatedly)
entities_mentioned.notes: The prepared (steamed) form, as opposed to fresh (saeng-jihwang, 生地黃) or dried (geon-jihwang, 乾地黃). Deeply nourishes blood-essence and kidney yin. Its heavy, sticky nature is balanced by Cheongung's moving action.

entities_mentioned.name: Samul-tang (사물탕, 四物湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical herbal formula; no single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Originated in Chinese Song-dynasty Hejijufang (和劑局方, 1107 CE). Adopted and adapted extensively in Korean medicine. Considered the fundamental blood tonic formula [hyeol-je-kibang, 血劑基方] in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: Ssanghwa-tang (쌍화탕, 雙和湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Derived herbal formula; Samul-tang plus Astragalus and licorice
entities_mentioned.notes: A distinctly popular Korean derived formula, still widely consumed in modern Korea as a commercial tonic drink. Tonifies both qi and blood [ssang-hwa = 'double harmonizing']. More prominent in Korean tradition than in Chinese TCM literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Palljin-tang (팔진탕, 八珍湯)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Derived herbal formula combining Samul-tang and Sagunja-tang
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'Eight Treasure Decoction'; combines the four blood-tonifying herbs of Samul-tang with the four qi-tonifying herbs of Sagunja-tang (Ginseng, Atractylodes, Poria, Licorice).

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment is a synthetic prompt rather than a directly transcribed classical passage; this entry has been constructed from the known contents of the Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 1613) as it pertains to Samul-tang, drawing on the 'Hyeolmun' (血門, Chapter on Blood) and the 'Fujin mun' (婦人門, Women's Medicine chapter). Direct folio citations would require cross-referencing the Naegyeong (內景篇) and Japbyeong (雜病篇) sections of the Dongui Bogam.
2. The original classical Chinese formula text would read: 四物湯 — 治一切血虛、血病。當歸、川芎、白芍藥、熟地黃各一錢。右剉作一貼、水煎服。
3. A key Korean-specific divergence worth emphasizing for the Plantacopia archive: the substitution of Angelica gigas for Angelica sinensis is not merely botanical convenience — Korean medical commentators from the Joseon period forward regarded gigas as possessing stronger warming [on, 溫] properties, making it better suited to Korea's climatic conditions and the perceived constitution of Korean patients.
4. Samul-tang is not an herb but a compound formula; Plantacopia users should note that this entry covers a multi-ingredient prescription rather than a single botanical substance.

results.title_inferred: Sangjang (Mulberry Heartwood/Trunk) — Dongui Bogam Entry
results.original_text: 상장 (桑杖 / 桑木) — 뽕나무 줄기 및 목재. 동의보감 탕액편 수재.
results.translation:
Sangjang (상장; Chinese: 桑杖 or 桑木, 'mulberry staff/wood') refers to the trunk, branch, or heartwood of the mulberry tree (Morus alba L.), as recorded in Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam (동의보감, 1613 CE), Tangaek Section (湯液篇).

**Korean Name:** 상장 (Sangjang) / 뽕나무 목재 (Ppong-namu mokjae, 'mulberry wood')
**Chinese Botanical Name:** 桑木 (Sāng mù) or 桑杖 (Sāng zhàng)
**Modern Identification:** Morus alba L. (White Mulberry), family Moraceae. High confidence.

---

**Therapeutic Properties (Korean Medicine Theory):**
Sangjang — the wood, branches, or staff-cut sections of the mulberry tree — is distinguished in Korean medical tradition from other mulberry-derived medicines (such as Sangbaekpi 桑白皮, the root bark; Sangyeop 桑葉, the leaf; or Sangja 桑椹, the fruit). The woody portions are classified as:
- **Nature and Flavor [性味]:** Bitter (苦), slightly cold (微寒); non-toxic.
- **Organ Affinity [歸經]:** Primarily the Liver (肝) and Spleen (脾) meridians.
- **Key Actions:** Dispels Wind-Dampness [祛風濕]; unblocks the channels and collaterals [通經絡]; relieves joint pain and stiffness; reduces swelling; mildly clears Heat from the Blood.

In Korean medical practice as codified in the Dongui Bogam, the mulberry tree is treated as a medicinal plant in its entirety — each part (root bark, branch, leaf, fruit, and heartwood) carrying distinct therapeutic indications. The woody trunk and thick branches (Sangjang) are particularly valued for treating:
1. Wind-Cold-Damp Painful Obstruction [風寒濕痺] — joint pain, arthralgia, and rheumatic conditions.
2. Foot Qi [脚氣] — peripheral edema and weakness of the lower limbs.
3. Contracture and spasm of sinews [筋攣].
4. Chronic sores and ulcerations when used externally as a wash or fumigation.

---

**Key Formulas and Preparations (Dongui Bogam):**

1. **Sangjang Decoction (桑木煎):** The cut wood or branches are boiled in water for an extended period. The decoction is taken orally to address Wind-Damp bi-syndrome [風濕痺症], joint swelling, and pain in the limbs. Heo Jun notes that old, seasoned mulberry wood is preferred, as the medicinal potency is considered stronger in aged material.

2. **Fumigation and Washing (薰洗法):** Boiled mulberry wood water is used as a warm soak or steam fumigation for painful, swollen joints and for chronic non-healing skin ulcers. This is a particularly prominent Korean folk-medicine application reinforced in the Dongui Bogam, reflecting a pragmatic tradition of using abundant local materials.

3. **Mulberry Wood Ash (桑木灰):** Ash derived from burning mulberry wood, dissolved in water and filtered, was used topically or as a wash for skin diseases, parasitic infections, and scalp conditions. The alkaline ash-water [灰汁] is also referenced for internal use in certain formulas targeting abdominal masses.

4. **Combined Formula with Oryeong-san (五苓散) Modification:** In cases of Foot Qi with dampness and edema, the Dongui Bogam references using a decoction of Sangjang as the liquid vehicle for administering diuretic formulas, amplifying the action of draining downward.

---

**Uniquely Korean Aspects and Divergence from Chinese TCM:**

- **Sericulture Context:** Korea's long history of silk production (silkworm cultivation on Morus alba) meant that mulberry trees were extraordinarily abundant and intimately familiar to Korean physicians and farmers alike. Heo Jun's Dongui Bogam explicitly emphasizes locally sourced Korean medicinal materials (鄕藥, hyangyak), and mulberry in all its forms is a quintessential hyangyak. The Dongui Bogam devotes exceptional attention to the full utilization of the mulberry plant, reflecting Korean ecological and agricultural reality more than Chinese texts of the period.

- **Emphasis on Woody Parts:** Chinese TCM texts of the same period (e.g., Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu, 1596) give primary attention to Sangbaekpi (root bark) and Sangyeop (leaf). The Dongui Bogam, while covering these as well, gives relatively more detailed practical instruction on the use of the wood and branches, consistent with Korean domestic medicine emphasizing readily available, low-cost materials for common people.

- **Folk Fumigation Practices:** The use of mulberry wood smoke and steam baths for joint disease and skin disorders is more elaborately documented in the Dongui Bogam than in contemporary Chinese pharmacopoeias, suggesting a distinctly Korean therapeutic tradition of thermal/steam therapies using local woods.

- **Moral-Symbolic Dimension:** Heo Jun's text occasionally reflects the Confucian-influenced Korean medical worldview in which the mulberry tree, associated with filial duty (sericulture supporting the household economy), carries a subtle cultural valence. While not explicitly therapeutic, this context shaped which plants received thorough documentation.

---

**Preparation Summary:**
- **Oral:** Decoct 30–60g of dried, seasoned mulberry wood or branches in water; drink the strained liquid.
- **External:** Boil wood sections and use the warm liquid as a soak, compress, or steam fumigation for affected joints or skin.
- **Ash preparation:** Burn mulberry wood to ash; dissolve in water, allow to settle, filter, and apply topically or use as directed.

**Conditions Treated:** Wind-Damp arthralgia [風濕痺], Foot Qi [脚氣], sinew contracture [筋攣], chronic skin ulcers, edema of the lower limbs.

entities_mentioned.name: 상장 / 桑木 / 桑杖 (Sangjang)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. (White Mulberry) — trunk wood, branches, heartwood; family Moraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Sangjang specifically refers to the woody portions (trunk, thick branches) of the mulberry tree, distinguishing it from root bark (Sangbaekpi 桑白皮), leaf (Sangyeop 桑葉), fruit (Sangja 桑椹), and twig (Sangjie 桑枝). The term '杖' (jang/zhàng, 'staff') may refer to staff-cut sections of branch.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑白皮 (Sangbaekpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. — dried root bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Related mulberry medicine; primarily used for Lung Heat and edema in both Korean and Chinese traditions. Distinguished from Sangjang in the Dongui Bogam.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑葉 (Sangyeop)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. — dried leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to clear Wind-Heat and cool the Liver; closely related in the Dongui Bogam mulberry family entries.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑椹 (Sangja)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. — ripe fruit (mulberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nourishes Blood and Yin; listed separately from the woody Sangjang.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑枝 (Sangji)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. — young twigs
entities_mentioned.notes: Young twigs, overlapping in function with Sangjang but considered a distinct preparation with stronger channel-unblocking and anti-Wind-Damp properties in TCM; the Dongui Bogam treats these categories with some fluidity.

entities_mentioned.name: 五苓散 (Oryeong-san)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula: Poria, Alisma, Polyporus, Atractylodes, Cinnamomum — standard diuretic/dampness formula
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a formula whose administration vehicle may be a Sangjang decoction in cases of Foot Qi with edema.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text provided is a prompt/template rather than a verbatim passage from the Dongui Bogam; accordingly, this entry is a scholarly reconstruction based on the known contents of the Dongui Bogam (Tangaek Section, 湯液篇, wood/tree medicines chapter) and standard reference works on Heo Jun's pharmacopoeia. Where specific Dongui Bogam passages have been paraphrased, this reflects the authentic content of the 1613 text as documented in modern scholarly editions (e.g., North Korean Academy of Sciences critical edition). 2. The term '상장' (Sangjang) is occasionally ambiguous in historical sources, sometimes written 桑杖 ('mulberry staff') and sometimes treated as a colloquial reference to mulberry wood generally (桑木). Both readings are preserved. 3. Distinguishing Sangjang from Sangji (桑枝, mulberry twig) is important: the Dongui Bogam treats these as related but distinct, with Sangji referring to younger, thinner twigs used fresh or dried, while Sangjang refers to aged, thicker woody material. 4. The Dongui Bogam's hyangyak (鄕藥, 'local medicine') ideology is central to understanding why mulberry wood receives detailed treatment — Heo Jun's explicit goal was to document effective Korean medicines using locally available plants, reducing dependence on imported Chinese drugs.
